Increase speed deviation threshold slightly if nuisance tripping
If PM motor, run motor auto-tune to re-learn motor characteristics
faults.field_trick: Run motor unloaded. If speed holds perfectly, the problem is mechanical load, not the drive.

faults.name: VFD Output Transistor Fault
faults.description: Drive output power transistor (IGBT) reported a desaturation or short-circuit fault. Output stage may be damaged.
probable_causes: IGBT module failure — end of life or surge damage
Output short circuit — motor cable pinched or shorted
Gate driver board failure — not controlling IGBTs properly
Power surge from lightning or switching transient
Motor with very low impedance — cable too short with large motor
fix_steps: Power down and wait 5 minutes for capacitor discharge
Disconnect motor cables at drive output
Use IGBT tester or diode check on multimeter across each output phase to DC bus
Each IGBT should show ~0.4V in one direction, OL in other
If any IGBT reads shorted, drive needs repair or replacement
Check motor cables for shorts before reconnecting
faults.field_trick: Diode-check from each output terminal to DC+ and DC-. You should see ~0.4V drop one way, OL the other. If any reads 0.0V both ways, that IGBT is shorted.

faults.name: VFD Instantaneous Output Fault
faults.description: Drive detected a sudden output phase current spike exceeding the instantaneous trip threshold.
probable_causes: Flying restart into spinning motor — voltage phase mismatch
Intermittent short in motor cable — arcing under load
Motor turn-to-turn short developing — insulation breakdown
Loose connection at motor terminals — arcing under vibration
Wrong motor type selected in drive parameters
fix_steps: If motor was spinning when drive started, enable catch-a-spinning-load feature
Megger motor cables and motor windings — check for developing short
Tighten all connections at drive output and motor terminal box
Verify motor nameplate data matches drive parameters exactly
If intermittent, monitor drive output current waveform for spikes
faults.field_trick: If the fault only happens on restart after a coast-stop, enable 'DC injection braking' or 'flying start catch'. The motor is still spinning when the drive tries to restart.

faults.name: VFD Analog Input Signal Loss
faults.description: 4-20mA or 0-10V analog speed reference signal dropped below minimum threshold or was lost entirely.
probable_causes: Analog signal wire broken or disconnected
Transmitter/controller sending the signal has faulted
Shield not grounded properly — electrical noise corrupting signal
Analog input terminal corrosion — high-resistance connection
Wrong signal type selected — drive expects 4-20mA but receiving 0-10V
fix_steps: Measure analog signal at drive input terminals with multimeter
For 4-20mA: should read 4mA minimum. Below 3.5mA = signal loss
For 0-10V: should read >0.5V normally. 0V = signal loss
Check wiring from source to drive — repair any breaks
Verify signal source is outputting correct range
Clean terminal connections — remove corrosion
Set drive fault-on-signal-loss action: coast, run at preset speed, or fault
faults.field_trick: Disconnect the signal wire at the drive and jumper a 9V battery across the analog input (with a 500 ohm resistor for 4-20mA). If the drive responds, your signal source is the problem.

faults.name: PowerFlex 525 F12 - HW OverCurrent
faults.description: PowerFlex 525 hardware overcurrent fault. Instantaneous current exceeded drive hardware limit.
probable_causes: Motor cable short circuit — phase-to-phase or phase-to-ground
Motor winding failure — sudden insulation breakdown
Drive IGBT failure — shorted output device
Starting into a spinning motor without catch-a-spinning-load enabled
Incorrect motor parameters causing excessive magnetizing current
fix_steps: Disconnect motor cables at drive and attempt to clear fault
If fault clears, megger motor cables and motor windings
If fault persists with motor disconnected, drive IGBTs may be damaged
Run IGBT diagnostic test if drive supports it
Verify motor parameters match nameplate (A103-A108 on PF525)
Enable flying start (A541) if motor may be spinning on restart
faults.field_trick: F12 on a PF525 with motor disconnected = dead drive. F12 only with motor connected = bad cable or motor.

faults.name: PowerFlex 525 F5 - OverVoltage
faults.description: PowerFlex 525 DC bus overvoltage fault. Regenerative energy or high line voltage caused bus voltage to exceed threshold.
probable_causes: Deceleration time too fast for load inertia
No dynamic braking resistor installed for high-inertia load
Input voltage above drive rating — measure at input terminals
Multiple drives on same bus regenerating simultaneously
Drive bus overvoltage prevention feature disabled
fix_steps: Increase deceleration time (parameter A403)
Enable bus voltage regulator (A541 on some models)
Install dynamic braking resistor if high-inertia load
Measure input voltage — must be within drive rating ±10%
Enable coast-to-stop if controlled decel not required
faults.field_trick: Set decel to 'Coast to Stop' (A404=0). If F5 goes away, you need a braking resistor or longer decel ramp.

faults.name: Kinetix Servo Drive Fault 16 - Excess Position Error
faults.description: Kinetix 5500/5700 servo reports excessive following error. Actual position is too far behind commanded position.
probable_causes: Servo tuning too aggressive or too loose for the load
Mechanical binding — axis cannot follow commanded profile
Encoder cable intermittent — losing position feedback momentarily
Load inertia mismatch — load much heavier than servo can handle
Servo coupling slipping — motor moves but load doesn't follow
fix_steps: Check axis properties in Logix — review position error threshold
Run servo tune/hookup test to auto-tune gains
Check for mechanical binding — move axis by hand with motor disabled
Inspect coupling between motor and load — tighten set screws
Verify encoder cable connections at motor and drive
Increase position error threshold if nuisance faulting on fast moves
faults.field_trick: Run a servo hookup test in Studio 5000. If the auto-tune can't stabilize, the mechanical coupling is the problem — not the servo or tuning.

faults.name: PLC Task Watchdog Expired
faults.description: A PLC task exceeded its configured watchdog timer. Program execution took longer than the allowed time window.
probable_causes: Program too complex for task execution rate
Infinite or near-infinite loop in ladder/structured text
Event task triggered faster than it can complete
Network communication stall blocking program execution
Add-on instruction (AOI) with excessive computation
fix_steps: Go online with Studio 5000 — check which task faulted
Review task properties: actual scan time vs watchdog timer
Increase watchdog timer if scan time is consistently close to limit
Optimize program — move non-critical logic to slower task
Check for loops that iterate too many times per scan
Verify event task trigger rate is reasonable
faults.field_trick: Trend the task scan time in Studio 5000. If it's normally 10ms but spikes to 200ms, look for conditional logic that runs heavy code on rare conditions.

faults.name: PowerFlex 755 F64 - Drive Overtemp
faults.description: PowerFlex 755 drive heatsink or power section temperature exceeded safe operating limit.
probable_causes: Cooling fan assembly failed — fan module LEDs will be red
Cabinet cooling insufficient — A/C unit not keeping up
Drive overloaded — running above continuous current rating
Adjacent drives generating excessive heat in cabinet
Air filter on cabinet intake clogged with dust
fix_steps: Check fan module LEDs — green=OK, red=fault, replace if red
Measure ambient temp in cabinet — should be <40°C (104°F)
Clean or replace cabinet air filter
Verify drive current vs rated current — derate if ambient >40°C
Check cabinet A/C or heat exchanger operation
Ensure cabinet doors are fully closed — no air bypass
faults.field_trick: PF755 fan modules are hot-swappable. Pull the red-LED fan module and swap in the spare. Back online in 2 minutes.

faults.name: Conveyor Roller Motor Fault
faults.description: Motorized drive roller (MDR) has faulted or stopped responding. Zone controller reports motor failure.
probable_causes: MDR motor burned out — winding failure from overload
Zone controller card failed — not sending power to motor
O-ring drive band broken between MDR and slave rollers
Motor jam — product wrapped around roller shaft
24VDC power supply to zone controller failed
fix_steps: Check zone controller LEDs — identify which zone is faulted
Verify 24VDC power supply voltage — must be 24V ±10%
Check O-ring belts between MDR and slave rollers — replace if broken
Inspect MDR for product wrapped around shaft — clear debris
Swap MDR with known-good unit to confirm motor failure
Replace zone controller card if motor tests good
faults.field_trick: Pull the O-ring belt off the MDR and spin the roller by hand. If it doesn't spin freely, the motor is seized. If it spins freely but doesn't run, check the zone card.

faults.name: Conveyor Merge Collision Detected
faults.description: Products collided at a conveyor merge point. Photoeye detected overlapping or jammed products at merge zone.
probable_causes: Merge timing incorrect — both lines releasing simultaneously
Gap between products too small — not enough space for merge
Photoeye at merge point dirty or misaligned
PLC merge logic error — priority not being enforced
Speed differential too large between merging conveyors
fix_steps: Clear jammed products from merge point
Verify merge photoeye — clean lens, check alignment
Review merge timing in PLC — adjust gap timer to prevent simultaneous release
Match conveyor speeds at merge — both should be same speed or receiving slightly faster
Check merge priority logic — one line must yield to other
Run at reduced speed to observe merge behavior
faults.field_trick: The receiving conveyor after the merge should run 10-15% faster than the feeding conveyors. This creates a natural gap and prevents pile-ups.

faults.name: Conveyor Transfer Pop-up Fault
faults.description: Pop-up transfer (right-angle transfer) failed to actuate or did not reach full up/down position in time.
probable_causes: Pneumatic cylinder air supply low or disconnected
Solenoid valve stuck or coil burned out
Position sensors not confirming up or down position
Mechanical obstruction — product stuck under pop-up section
Cylinder seals worn — slow or weak actuation
fix_steps: Check air pressure at transfer — gauge should read plant spec
Manually actuate solenoid — use override button on valve body
Check position confirm sensors — clean and adjust as needed
Inspect under pop-up for trapped products or debris
If cylinder slow, check flow control valves — may be closed too much
Check PLC output — verify output LED is ON when transfer commanded
faults.field_trick: Hit the solenoid manual override. If the transfer pops up, the problem is electrical (PLC output or solenoid driver). If nothing happens, check air first, then the cylinder.

faults.name: Checkweigher Reject
faults.description: Inline checkweigher detected product outside acceptable weight range. Product rejected from line.
probable_causes: Underfill — product doesn't meet minimum weight specification
Overfill — product exceeds maximum weight specification
Checkweigher calibration drifted — readings inaccurate
Vibration from adjacent equipment affecting weight reading
Draft/air current affecting scale accuracy
fix_steps: Check rejected product on bench scale — verify actual weight
If weight is actually in spec, recalibrate checkweigher with test weights
Adjust fill machine if underfill/overfill is confirmed
Check for vibration — isolate checkweigher from conveyor vibration
Install draft shields if air currents are affecting readings
Review reject rate trend — sudden increase indicates filling problem
faults.field_trick: Run 10 known-weight test packs through the checkweigher. If they all read within ±1g of actual, the checkweigher is fine and your filler is the problem.

faults.name: Heat Seal Temperature Out of Range
faults.description: Heat seal bar temperature is above or below the specified sealing range. Seal quality may be compromised.
probable_causes: Heater element failing — resistance increasing, output decreasing
Thermocouple failed or dislodged — reading incorrect temperature
Temperature controller fault — check for error display
Insulation around seal bar degraded — heat loss to surroundings
SSR (Solid State Relay) failed — stuck on or stuck off
fix_steps: Check actual temperature with external thermocouple/IR thermometer
Compare reading to controller display — if different, thermocouple is bad
Measure heater element resistance — compare to spec
Check SSR — if failed ON, temperature will overshoot continuously
If failed OFF, heater won't energize — replace SSR
Inspect thermocouple wiring — reseat connections at controller
faults.field_trick: Touch the SSR heatsink. If it's extremely hot with the heater off, the SSR is shorted ON. Replace it before it damages the seal bar.

faults.name: Carton Sealer Tape Fault
faults.description: Case/carton tape sealer reports tape out, tape jam, or tape application failure.
probable_causes: Tape roll empty — needs replacement
Tape wrapped around cutting blade — blade dull or misaligned
Tape not adhering — wrong tape grade for carton material or temperature
Tape tension too tight or too loose — feeding incorrectly
Roller assembly dirty — tape sticking to rollers instead of carton
fix_steps: Replace tape roll if empty — use correct width and grade
Clean tape path and cutting blade — remove any tape residue
Replace cutting blade if dull — blade should have clean edge
Adjust tape tension — should feed smoothly without dragging
Clean all rollers in tape path with solvent
Verify carton flaps are flat and clean where tape is applied
If tape not sticking, try different tape grade or heat-assist tape
faults.field_trick: If tape wraps around the blade, the blade is dull. Period. Replace the blade and the problem stops.

faults.name: AS/RS WMS Communication Timeout
faults.description: AS/RS crane controller lost communication with Warehouse Management System. Cannot receive or confirm storage/retrieval commands.
probable_causes: WMS server down or unresponsive
Network switch between crane controller and WMS failed
WMS database lock or timeout — query taking too long
Firewall rule blocking crane-to-WMS traffic
API endpoint changed during WMS update
fix_steps: Check WMS application status — restart if needed
Verify network connectivity — ping WMS server from crane controller
Check network switches in the path — verify port LEDs
Review firewall rules if recently changed
Check WMS event log for errors at the time of communication loss
Restart crane controller communication service
faults.field_trick: Ping the WMS server continuously while the crane runs. If pings succeed but commands fail, it's the WMS application, not the network.

faults.name: AS/RS Barcode Read Failure
faults.description: Barcode scanner at crane or conveyor station failed to read pallet or product barcode. Load cannot be identified for storage.
probable_causes: Barcode label damaged, wrinkled, or missing
Scanner lens dirty — reduced read distance or reliability
Label orientation wrong — barcode not facing scanner
Scanner out of focus — focal distance changed due to mounting vibration
Ambient light interference — direct sunlight or reflection
fix_steps: Inspect barcode label — replace if damaged, missing, or wrinkled
Clean scanner lens with soft cloth
Verify label is positioned to face scanner at correct angle
Check scanner mounting — tighten if loose, verify focal distance
Test with known-good barcode in same position
If ambient light issue, install a light shield or switch to laser scanner
faults.field_trick: Hold a test barcode (printed fresh, known good) in front of the scanner. If it reads the test but not the pallet label, it's the label quality, not the scanner.

faults.name: Safety PLC Firmware Mismatch
faults.description: Safety controller firmware version does not match the safety program requirements. Safety functions may not execute correctly.
probable_causes: Firmware updated on one controller in redundant pair but not the other
Safety program developed on different firmware version
Firmware downgrade attempted without matching program revision
Replacement processor has factory firmware that's too old
fix_steps: Check current firmware version vs program requirement in safety task properties
Update firmware to match program requirement using ControlFlash
If redundant pair, update BOTH processors to same firmware version
Verify safety signature after firmware update
Re-validate safety function after any firmware change per site safety plan
faults.field_trick: The safety task properties in Studio 5000 tell you EXACTLY what firmware version it needs. Compare that to what's on the processor. Match them and the fault clears.

faults.name: Emergency Stop Circuit Ground Fault
faults.description: Ground fault detected in the E-stop safety circuit. Circuit integrity compromised — machine locked out.
probable_causes: E-stop wiring insulation damaged — wire touching conduit or enclosure
Moisture in junction box shorting wire to ground
E-stop button contact block insulation failure
Conduit nipple cutting through wire insulation over time
Cable gland not sealing — water ingress in wet environment
fix_steps: Use ground fault locator or megger to find fault location in circuit
Section the circuit — disconnect at junction boxes to isolate the faulted segment
Inspect wiring at each junction box for damaged insulation or moisture
Check wire routing through conduit — look for sharp edges cutting insulation
Replace damaged wire — use correct voltage rating for safety circuit
Seal all junction boxes and cable entries against moisture
faults.field_trick: Disconnect the E-stop circuit at the halfway point. Megger each half. The half that reads low to ground has the fault. Keep halving until you find it.

faults.name: Pneumatic Cylinder Slow or Weak
faults.description: Pneumatic cylinder not extending or retracting at normal speed, or not developing full force.
probable_causes: Air supply pressure low — main line or local regulator
Flow control valve closed too far — restricting cylinder speed
Cylinder rod seal leaking — air bypassing piston internally
Solenoid valve not fully shifting — partial flow only
Exhaust port restricted — muffler clogged with oil/debris
fix_steps: Check air pressure at the cylinder — should match spec (typically 80-90 PSI)
Open flow controls slightly — adjust speed without sacrificing control
Listen for air leaking from exhaust when cylinder should be holding position
Check solenoid valve spool — clean or replace if sticking
Clean or replace exhaust mufflers
If cylinder seals leaking (rod slowly drifts), rebuild or replace cylinder
faults.field_trick: Put your hand over the exhaust port on the solenoid when the cylinder is holding position. If you feel air, the cylinder seals are leaking and it needs a rebuild.

faults.name: Hydraulic System Low Pressure
faults.description: Hydraulic power unit pressure below minimum for equipment operation. Cylinders and motors cannot develop full force.
probable_causes: Hydraulic pump worn — internal leakage reducing output flow
Relief valve set too low or stuck partially open
Oil level low in reservoir — air being sucked into pump
Oil filter clogged — restricting flow to system
Internal cylinder leak — oil bypassing piston seals
Hydraulic hose burst or fitting leaking
fix_steps: Check reservoir oil level — fill to proper mark with correct grade
Check system pressure gauge — compare to spec
Inspect for leaks — hoses, fittings, cylinders, and around pump shaft seal
Replace hydraulic filter if pressure drop across filter exceeds indicator
Test relief valve — adjust to spec or replace if stuck
Check pump output — low flow at correct RPM = worn pump
Check oil temperature — overheating thins oil and reduces pressure
faults.field_trick: Clamp off one cylinder at a time and check if pressure recovers. When you clamp the leaking cylinder, pressure comes back. That's your bad seal.

faults.name: Excessive Vibration Alarm
faults.description: Vibration monitoring sensor detected amplitude exceeding alarm threshold on monitored equipment.
probable_causes: Motor or pump bearings failing — vibration increases as bearings degrade
Coupling misalignment between motor and driven equipment
Imbalance in rotating component — fan, impeller, or flywheel
Loose foundation bolts — equipment walking on base
Belt or chain whip from incorrect tension
fix_steps: Check vibration data — is it axial, radial, or both?
Radial dominant = imbalance. Axial dominant = misalignment.
Tighten all foundation/mounting bolts
Check coupling alignment with dial indicators or laser alignment tool
Check belt tension — too loose causes whip, too tight causes bearing load
If bearing-related, schedule bearing replacement before catastrophic failure
Trend vibration data — rising trend confirms degradation
faults.field_trick: Put a penny on top of the motor. If the penny bounces off, vibration is excessive and bearings are probably going. If it stays, vibration is within acceptable range.

faults.name: Process Temperature Out of Range
faults.description: Process temperature sensor reading is outside configured high or low alarm limits.
probable_causes: Heater element burned out or partially failed
Thermocouple/RTD sensor failed — reading wrong temperature
PID controller output saturated — unable to maintain setpoint
Cooling system failure — unable to remove enough heat
Process load changed — more or less thermal mass than normal
fix_steps: Verify actual temperature with external measurement (IR gun or portable probe)
Compare external reading to sensor reading — if different, sensor is bad
Check heater element — measure resistance and compare to spec
Verify PID controller output — should be modulating, not stuck at 0% or 100%
Check cooling system — fans, pumps, valves operating correctly
Review PID tuning if temperature is oscillating around setpoint
faults.field_trick: Check the PID output percentage. If it's pegged at 100% and temp is low, you've lost heating capacity. If it's at 0% and temp is high, you've lost cooling.

faults.name: Ground Fault on Power Circuit
faults.description: Ground fault relay or GFCI detected leakage current to ground on a power circuit. Circuit tripped for safety.
probable_causes: Motor winding insulation failure — current leaking to frame
Cable insulation damaged — water ingress, physical damage, or age
VFD output filter capacitor leaking to ground
Moisture in motor terminal box or junction box
Heater element insulation breakdown
fix_steps: Identify which circuit tripped — check ground fault relay indicator
Disconnect loads one at a time to isolate faulted circuit
Megger each load and its cable: >1 MΩ phase-to-ground = good
Check for moisture in junction boxes, motor terminals, and conduit runs
If motor, may need drying out or rewinding
If cable, repair or replace damaged section
Check ground fault relay sensitivity — may need adjustment for VFD circuits
faults.field_trick: Disconnect all loads from the circuit. Reset the GFR. If it holds, reconnect loads one at a time. The one that trips it is your ground fault.

faults.name: Palletizer Slip Sheet Fault
faults.description: Slip sheet or tier sheet dispenser failed to pick or place a sheet between pallet layers.
probable_causes: Sheet magazine empty — needs refill
Vacuum cups not gripping sheet — cups worn or dirty
Sheets stuck together — static or moisture causing adhesion
Sheet size incorrect — wrong size loaded in magazine
Vacuum pump pressure insufficient
fix_steps: Refill sheet magazine if empty
Check vacuum cups — replace if worn, cracked, or deformed
Fan/shuffle sheets before loading to separate stuck sheets
Verify sheet size matches machine configuration
Check vacuum pump operation and gauge reading
Clean vacuum cups and sheet contact surfaces
faults.field_trick: Sheets sticking together is the #1 slip sheet problem. Fan the stack like a deck of cards before loading. Eliminates 90% of pick failures.

faults.name: Palletizer Robot Teach Point Error
faults.description: Robotic palletizer position data does not match expected coordinates. Teach points may have shifted.
probable_causes: Robot encoder lost position after emergency stop or power loss
Teach pendant values accidentally modified
End-of-arm tool (EOAT) mounting shifted or damaged
Pallet or conveyor position changed — physical reference moved
Robot calibration data corrupted
fix_steps: Home/master the robot to re-establish reference positions
Jog robot to known teach point and compare position data
Check EOAT mounting — tighten bolts, verify alignment
Verify pallet and conveyor positions haven't shifted
Re-teach affected points if positions have drifted
Back up teach data regularly to prevent loss
faults.field_trick: Touch up ONE teach point (like the pallet corner) and run one cycle in slow mode. If it's off, all points shifted equally and you just need to re-master, not re-teach everything.

faults.name: Piler Clamp Pressure Low
faults.description: Piler clamp arm hydraulic or pneumatic pressure below minimum for safe product handling. Risk of dropping load.
probable_causes: Hydraulic/pneumatic supply pressure low
Clamp cylinder seal leaking — pressure bleeds off
Pressure regulator set too low or failed
Pressure sensor fault — reading lower than actual
Hose or fitting leak in clamp circuit
fix_steps: Check supply pressure at clamp circuit — must meet spec
Look for oil/air leaks at cylinder, hoses, and fittings
Verify pressure regulator setting — adjust to spec
Test pressure sensor with calibrated gauge — compare readings
If cylinder leaking internally, rebuild or replace
faults.field_trick: Watch the pressure gauge with the clamp closed on a load. If pressure slowly drops, the clamp cylinder is leaking internally. If it holds steady, check the sensor.

faults.name: Piler Load Presence Sensor Fault
faults.description: Load presence sensor on piler forks or platform not confirming product presence when expected, or showing false presence.
probable_causes: Sensor dirty — product dust or debris on lens
Sensor misaligned — vibration shifted mounting
Product not positioned correctly on forks/platform
Sensor wire damaged or disconnected
Sensor sensitivity set wrong for product type
fix_steps: Clean sensor lens with dry cloth
Verify product is positioned within sensor detection zone
Realign sensor — use indicator LED on sensor body for feedback
Check wiring connections at sensor and PLC input
Adjust sensor sensitivity for the specific product being handled
Replace sensor if cleaning and alignment don't resolve
faults.field_trick: Block the sensor by hand. If the PLC input responds, the sensor works — product just isn't in the right spot. If PLC doesn't respond, check wiring.

faults.name: VFD Current Fault During Accel
faults.description: Drive faulted on overcurrent during acceleration ramp. Motor drawing excessive current trying to accelerate the load.
probable_causes: Acceleration time too short for the load inertia
Motor undersized for the application
Mechanical binding during startup
Drive rated current too low for motor
Motor magnetics saturating at low frequency
fix_steps: Increase acceleration time — double current value and test
Check motor FLA vs drive rated current — drive must be >= motor FLA
Verify no mechanical binding during startup — try starting unloaded
Enable torque limit during acceleration if drive supports it
Check motor boost voltage parameter — reduce if motor saturating
faults.field_trick: Double the accel time. If it stops faulting, split the difference to find the minimum safe ramp time.

faults.name: VFD EEPROM Write Error
faults.description: Drive failed to write parameters to non-volatile EEPROM memory. Parameter changes may not be retained after power cycle.
probable_causes: EEPROM reaching end of write-cycle life — limited write endurance
Power interruption during parameter save
Excessive automatic parameter saves — wearing EEPROM prematurely
EEPROM chip failure
fix_steps: Retry parameter save — some drives require manual save command
Reduce automatic save frequency if application writes parameters often
Save current parameters to external keypad memory as backup
If persistent, drive EEPROM may need factory repair
Record all custom parameters on paper as emergency backup
faults.field_trick: Stop auto-saving parameters after every change. Batch your changes, then do ONE manual save. EEPROM has limited write cycles — burning them on auto-save kills drives.

faults.name: VFD Safe Torque Off Active
faults.description: Drive Safe Torque Off (STO) function is active. Drive output is disabled by safety circuit — motor cannot run.
probable_causes: STO input not energized — safety circuit open
Safety relay output feeding STO is tripped
E-stop circuit active — removing STO enable signal
STO wiring disconnected or broken
STO terminal not jumpered when safety function not used
fix_steps: Check STO terminal — should have 24VDC when safe to run
Trace STO wiring back to safety relay or PLC safety output
If STO not used, jumper the STO terminals per drive manual
Reset safety relay if tripped — investigate why it tripped
Check E-stop circuit — all stations released and circuit complete
faults.field_trick: Put your meter on the STO terminals. 24V = safe to run, should start. 0V = safety circuit open, trace it back.

faults.name: PanelView HMI Application Fault
faults.description: PanelView or PanelView Plus HMI encountered an application fault. Screen may be frozen or displaying error.
probable_causes: Application file corrupted — bad download or storage fault
Memory full — logs, alarms, or data files consuming all RAM
Communication overload — too many tag updates per second
SD card failure — if application runs from SD card
Firmware incompatible with application version
fix_steps: Power-cycle the HMI — many application faults clear on restart
If stuck on boot, reload application from backup via USB or SD card
Check SD card — replace if card read errors in diagnostic log
Verify firmware vs application version compatibility
Clear alarm and data logs if memory is full
Reduce tag update rates for non-critical screens
faults.field_trick: Keep a USB drive with the HMI application backup taped to the inside of the panel door. When the HMI crashes at 2 AM, the backup is right there.

faults.name: PowerFlex 755 F81 - Drive HW Incompatible
faults.description: PowerFlex 755 detected hardware incompatibility. Power section and control section firmware or hardware revision mismatch.
probable_causes: Control module replaced with different hardware revision
Power section replaced with different revision
Firmware updated on control but not compatible with power hardware
Main control board was swapped from a different frame size
fix_steps: Check hardware catalog numbers on control and power modules — must be compatible
Update firmware to match both modules using ControlFlash
If modules from different drives, verify frame size compatibility
Contact Rockwell for compatibility matrix for your specific catalog numbers
Do NOT force firmware if hardware revisions don't match — can brick the drive
faults.field_trick: Write the firmware version on tape and stick it on both the control pod and the power section. When you replace one, you know what version the other is.

faults.name: ControlLogix EtherNet/IP Module CIP Error
faults.description: 1756-EN2T or EN3TR Ethernet module reporting CIP (Common Industrial Protocol) connection errors with remote devices.
probable_causes: Too many connections configured through this module — exceeds connection limit
Remote device not responding within RPI timeout
Network congestion — excessive broadcast or multicast traffic
Module firmware needs update for newer CIP features
Incorrect IP configuration causing routing issues
fix_steps: Check module diagnostics — connection count vs maximum (typically 128-256)
Distribute connections across multiple Ethernet modules if at limit
Increase RPI on non-critical connections to reduce network load
Update module firmware to latest available for your revision
Check network switch for excessive broadcasts — enable IGMP snooping
Verify subnet mask and gateway on all devices
faults.field_trick: Check the module connection count in Module Properties. If you're over 80% of max connections, add another EN2T module and spread the load.

faults.name: GuardLogix Safety Signature Mismatch
faults.description: GuardLogix safety controller reports safety signature does not match expected value. Safety program may have been modified without validation.
probable_causes: Safety program was edited but not re-validated
Safety signature was recorded incorrectly in safety documentation
Downloaded wrong safety program version
Safety add-on instruction (AOI) was modified
fix_steps: Compare current safety signature to documented approved signature
If program was intentionally modified, complete safety validation per site procedure
If unintentional, reload the approved safety program version from backup
Record new safety signature in safety documentation after validation
Lock safety task after validation to prevent unauthorized changes
faults.field_trick: Safety signature must match what's on the validation document. If it doesn't match, someone changed the safety program. Lock it down and investigate.

faults.name: Conveyor Brake Fault
faults.description: Conveyor holding brake failed to engage or release. Incline/decline conveyors require brake for load holding.
probable_causes: Brake coil burned out — cannot release electromagnetically
Brake air supply lost (air-release brakes)
Brake disc worn beyond adjustment range
Brake voltage incorrect — 24V brake getting 12V or vice versa
Brake wiring disconnected or contactor coil failed
fix_steps: Check brake type — spring-set/electrically released or air-released
For electric release: verify voltage at brake coil when energized
For air release: check air supply pressure at brake
Inspect brake disc for wear — measure and compare to minimum spec
Check brake release contactor — must energize when motor starts
If disc worn, adjust or replace — do NOT operate without brake on incline
faults.field_trick: Incline conveyor brakes are spring-set, power-release. If the conveyor creeps downhill when stopped, the brake disc is worn. Don't wait — replace before product slides.

faults.name: Conveyor Indexing Position Error
faults.description: Indexing conveyor stopped at wrong position. Product not aligned with station for filling, labeling, or other operations.
probable_causes: Index encoder missed pulses or lost reference
Clutch/brake unit slipping — not stopping precisely
Index photoeye dirty or misaligned — not confirming position
PLC index counter error — missed or double-counted products
Motor drive deceleration profile not tuned for indexing accuracy
fix_steps: Verify index sensor — clean and align position detection photoeye
Check encoder — verify pulse count matches physical positions
If clutch/brake, check air pressure and disc wear
Review PLC index counter logic — verify timing with product flow
Tune servo or VFD deceleration for crisp stop at index position
Check mechanical alignment of index pins or tooling
faults.field_trick: Put a mark on the belt at the exact station position. Run 10 index cycles and see how far the mark drifts. More than 1/4 inch? Tune the decel or check the clutch.

faults.name: AS/RS Rack Upright Damage Detected
faults.description: Rack inspection or sensor system detected damage to a rack upright or beam. Structural integrity may be compromised.
probable_causes: Forklift impact on rack upright — dent or bend visible
Overloaded shelf exceeded beam capacity — beam deflecting
Crane mast contacted rack during travel — misalignment
Seismic event or building settling shifted rack
Corrosion in wet environment weakening structural members
fix_steps: Immediately remove all loads from damaged rack section
Install rack protection barriers to prevent further damage
Have structural engineer or rack manufacturer assess damage
Do NOT load damaged rack until repaired and approved
If crane contact caused damage, check crane rail alignment
Document damage with photos for insurance and engineering review
faults.field_trick: If you can see a visible bend in a rack upright, that section is compromised. Unload it immediately. A loaded rack with a damaged upright can collapse without warning.

faults.name: AS/RS Pallet Overhang Detected
faults.description: Pallet overhang sensor detected product extending beyond the pallet edge. Overhang could catch on rack or crane during storage.
probable_causes: Product loaded past pallet edge by warehouse personnel
Shrink wrap shifted during transport to AS/RS
Pallet smaller than standard — product hangs over edge
Sensor calibration drift — reading false overhang
Non-standard pallet orientation at infeed
fix_steps: Reject pallet to manual rework station for repalletizing
Verify pallet dimensions match AS/RS specification
Check overhang sensors — calibrate with test pallet
Train warehouse team on pallet loading standards
Add pallet dimension check at AS/RS infeed as preventive measure
faults.field_trick: Put a straight edge on the pallet side. Anything sticking past the edge by more than 1 inch is an overhang that will catch in the rack. Fix it before storing.

faults.name: Shrink Tunnel Temperature Alarm
faults.description: Shrink tunnel heating section temperature exceeds setpoint or falls below minimum for proper film shrink.
probable_causes: Heater element burned out — zone running cold
Temperature controller output stuck — SSR failed
Thermocouple dislodged from tunnel wall
Blower motor failed — heat not circulating evenly
Product blocking airflow through tunnel
fix_steps: Check temperature at each zone with external probe
Verify all heater elements — measure resistance on each
Check thermocouple mounting — must be in good thermal contact
Verify blower motors are running — air must circulate for even heating
Clear any product jams inside tunnel
Check SSR operation — use clamp meter on heater circuit
faults.field_trick: Run your hand along the tunnel exit (carefully). If one side is hot and the other cold, a heater element or blower on the cold side has failed.

faults.name: Filling Machine Dose Error
faults.description: Filling machine output volume or weight deviates from target by more than allowable tolerance.
probable_causes: Fill nozzle partially clogged — restricted flow
Product viscosity changed — temperature or batch variation
Piston or pump seal worn — not delivering full stroke volume
Fill speed too fast — product splashing or foaming
Level sensor in hopper reading incorrectly — inconsistent supply
fix_steps: Collect and weigh 10 fills — determine if consistently over/under or random
Clean fill nozzles — disassemble and flush
Check product temperature — viscosity affects fill volume
Inspect piston seal or pump diaphragm — replace if worn
Reduce fill speed if foaming or splashing
Calibrate level sensor in product hopper
faults.field_trick: If fills are consistently low, check the nozzle for buildup. If fills are random high/low, check the pump seal — it's probably leaking air.

faults.name: Bottle Capper Torque Out of Spec
faults.description: Cap tightening torque is above or below specification. Caps may be loose (leak risk) or over-tightened (damage risk).
probable_causes: Torque setting drifted — needs recalibration
Capping head clutch worn — not holding target torque
Cap or bottle thread variation — different supplier lot
Capping head speed mismatch with conveyor — skipping threads
Capping chuck worn — not gripping cap securely
fix_steps: Test torque on 5 samples with calibrated torque meter
Adjust torque setting to center of specification
Inspect capping chuck — replace if worn smooth
Check cap supply — different lots may have thread variation
Verify capping head speed matches conveyor speed
Lubricate clutch mechanism per manufacturer schedule
faults.field_trick: If caps are cross-threading, slow the capper down. Cross-threading is almost always a speed issue — the cap doesn't have time to align before the torque hits.

faults.name: Area Scanner Zone Violation
faults.description: Safety laser scanner detected an object or person in the warning or protective zone. Machine speed reduced or stopped.
probable_causes: Person entered protected zone — normal safety function
Product or material fell into scanner zone
Scanner lens dirty — false detection
Reflective surface in zone causing mirror effect
Scanner mounting shifted — zone no longer aligned correctly
fix_steps: Verify zone is clear — remove any objects or materials
Clean scanner lens with microfiber cloth
Check for reflective surfaces — apply matte tape to eliminate reflections
Verify scanner mounting — tighten and realign if shifted
Review zone configuration — ensure protected zone matches physical guarding
Reset scanner after clearing the zone violation
faults.field_trick: Most scanners have a diagnostic display showing what they 'see'. Use it to identify exactly what triggered the zone violation — usually something you didn't expect, like a forklift reflector.

faults.name: Trapped Key Interlock Sequence Error
faults.description: Trapped key interlock sequence violation. Keys not exchanged in the correct order for safe access to hazardous area.
probable_causes: Operator tried to bypass correct key exchange sequence
Key stuck in lock — mechanism jammed
Lock mechanism worn — key extracts without full actuation
Extra key in circulation — duplicate compromises interlock sequence
New personnel unfamiliar with key exchange procedure
fix_steps: Follow correct key exchange sequence per lockout procedure sign
If key stuck, do NOT force — lubricate lock mechanism
Inspect lock mechanism — replace if key extracts without full travel
Audit key inventory — eliminate any duplicate keys
Retrain personnel on trapped key interlock procedure
Label all locks and keys clearly with sequence numbers
faults.field_trick: If someone 'lost' a trapped key, you have a SERIOUS problem. That key could be in someone's pocket, bypassing the interlock. Replace the entire key set.

faults.name: Motor Bearing Temperature High
faults.description: Motor bearing temperature sensor (RTD or thermocouple) reading above warning threshold. Bearing failure may be developing.
probable_causes: Bearing lubrication inadequate — dry or contaminated grease
Bearing wear — end of life approaching
Misalignment between motor and driven equipment
Over-greasing — excess grease causing heat
Bearing current damage from VFD — electrical discharge machining (EDM)
fix_steps: Compare bearing temp to opposite end bearing — both should be similar
Check lubrication schedule — add grease per manufacturer spec
Do NOT over-grease: use calculated volume, not 'until it squeezes out'
Check alignment — misalignment loads bearings unevenly
If VFD-driven, install shaft grounding ring to prevent bearing current damage
Schedule bearing replacement if temperature is trending up consistently
faults.field_trick: If one bearing is 20°F hotter than the other on the same motor, that's your problem bearing. The hot one is failing.

faults.name: PLC Power Supply Overload
faults.description: PLC chassis power supply is overloaded. Total current draw from modules exceeds power supply capacity.
probable_causes: Too many modules in chassis for power supply size
Module added without checking power budget
Power supply degrading — output voltage drooping under load
Field device backfed through I/O module drawing extra current
Short circuit on 24VDC field power distributed from chassis
fix_steps: Calculate total power draw of all modules vs power supply rating
If over budget, replace with higher-capacity power supply
Or redistribute modules across two chassis
Check power supply output voltage under load — should be within spec
Verify no field devices are backfeeding power through I/O modules
Check 24VDC distribution wiring for shorts
faults.field_trick: Add up the power consumption for each module from the catalog specs. If you're over 80% of the power supply rating, you need a bigger supply or fewer modules.

faults.name: Instrument Calibration Overdue
faults.description: Calibration interval has expired on one or more process instruments. Measurement accuracy cannot be guaranteed.
probable_causes: Calibration schedule not followed — maintenance gap
Instrument replaced but calibration date not updated
Calibration interval set too short — consuming resources unnecessarily
Calibration management system not alerting properly
fix_steps: Identify which instruments are overdue from calibration schedule
Prioritize safety-critical instruments (pressure relief, temperature limits)
Perform calibration using NIST-traceable standards
Update calibration date and results in management system
Review calibration interval — extend if instrument has been stable
faults.field_trick: Check the sticker on the instrument. If the 'next cal due' date has passed, that instrument is out of certification. Easy to miss if nobody checks.

faults.name: Cooling Water Temperature High
faults.description: Cooling water supply temperature exceeds maximum for equipment cooling requirements. Equipment may overheat.
probable_causes: Cooling tower fans not running or underperforming
Chiller compressor faulted or refrigerant low
Cooling water flow reduced — pump issue or valve closed
Heat load increased — more equipment running than cooling system designed for
Fouling in heat exchanger — scale or biological growth
fix_steps: Check cooling tower/chiller status — restart if faulted
Verify cooling water flow — check pump operation and valve positions
Measure supply vs return temperatures — high delta-T = flow too low
Inspect heat exchangers for fouling — clean if needed
Verify cooling tower fan operation — all fans should be running in summer
If temporary, reduce production heat load until cooling is restored
faults.field_trick: Feel the cooling water supply and return lines. If both are hot, your cooling source (tower/chiller) is the problem. If supply is cool but return is very hot, you have a flow restriction.

faults.name: Dust Collection System Alarm
faults.description: Dust collection system reports high differential pressure, low airflow, or bin full alarm.
probable_causes: Filter bags or cartridges clogged — need cleaning or replacement
Dust collection bin full — needs emptying
Ductwork blocked or collapsed
Fan motor or belt failure — reduced airflow
Pulse-jet cleaning system not functioning
fix_steps: Check differential pressure gauge — high reading = clogged filters
Empty dust collection bin if at capacity
Inspect ductwork for blockages — check blast gates are open
Verify fan motor operation and belt condition
Check pulse-jet system — solenoid valves, compressed air supply, timer
Replace filters if cleaning cycle cannot restore airflow
faults.field_trick: Put your hand near a pickup point. If you don't feel suction, either the blast gate is closed, the duct is blocked, or the fan has failed. Work from the pickup back to the fan.

faults.name: Stratix Switch Port Fault
faults.description: Stratix managed switch reports a port error, CRC errors, or excessive collisions on an Ethernet port.
probable_causes: Cable damage — causing bit errors and CRC failures
Cable too long — exceeding 100m Cat5e/Cat6 maximum
Incorrect cable type — using crossover instead of straight-through
EMI interference — Ethernet cable routed near power cables
Port hardware failure on the switch
fix_steps: Check cable with Ethernet cable tester — verify all 8 wires pass
Replace cable if CRC errors persist — use Cat6 shielded in industrial environments
Verify cable length — must be under 100m (328ft) between devices
Route Ethernet cables at least 6 inches from power cables
If port fault, move device to different port to isolate
Check switch port statistics — rising error count indicates cable issue
faults.field_trick: CRC errors = bad cable. Always. Check the cable, replace it, move on. Don't waste time troubleshooting the switch or the device on the other end.

faults.name: DeviceNet Scanner Communication Fault
faults.description: DeviceNet scanner module lost communication with one or more nodes on the DeviceNet network.
probable_causes: DeviceNet cable damage — trunk or drop cable
Node powered off or in fault
Node address conflict — duplicate MAC IDs
Termination resistor missing on one end of trunk
Bus power supply failure — 24V on network dropped
fix_steps: Check scanner I/O tree — identify which node(s) lost communication
Verify node power and status LEDs
Check DeviceNet cable connections — reseat at problem node
Verify termination resistors on both ends of the trunk line
Check network power supply — must be 24VDC ±5%
Use DeviceNet network health analyzer if available
faults.field_trick: DeviceNet problems are almost always wiring. Check for the two 121-ohm termination resistors first — one missing resistor can take down the whole network.

faults.name: Conveyor Spiral/Helix Fault
faults.description: Spiral or helix conveyor system fault. Belt or chain in spiral section reporting tension, tracking, or speed issue.
probable_causes: Belt tension incorrect — too tight causes motor overload, too loose causes slipping
Chain lubrication dry in spiral section — increased friction
Product jam in spiral — stuck product stops belt movement
Drive chain or sprocket wear — elongation causing position errors
Ambient temperature change affecting belt length
fix_steps: Check belt/chain tension — adjust per manufacturer spec for operating temperature
Inspect spiral section for product jams — clear from inside if accessible
Verify lubrication system is delivering to spiral chain guides
Check drive sprocket and chain wear — replace if chain >3% elongated
If seasonal temp change, readjust tension for current conditions
Run spiral empty to verify smooth operation before adding product
faults.field_trick: Spiral conveyors tighten in cold weather and loosen in hot weather. If you have tension problems that come and go seasonally, install an automatic tension adjustment.

faults.name: Palletizer Gripper Release Failure
faults.description: Palletizer gripper or clamp did not release product at the placement position. Product still held after release command.
probable_causes: Vacuum release valve stuck — still holding suction
Clamp cylinder not retracting — air supply or valve issue
Release sensor not confirming open position
Product adhered to gripper surface — sticky or wet product
Blow-off air not functioning — vacuum needs positive break
fix_steps: Check gripper release mechanism — vacuum blow-off or clamp retract
Verify solenoid valve for release — manual override test
Check blow-off air pressure — should be sufficient to break vacuum seal
Clean gripper contact surfaces — remove product residue
Inspect release position sensors — clean and adjust
If vacuum system, verify blow-off timer is long enough
faults.field_trick: If vacuum grippers don't release, the blow-off isn't strong enough. Increase blow-off duration by 0.1 second at a time until release is clean.

faults.name: Palletizer Infeed Backup
faults.description: Products backing up on palletizer infeed conveyor. Palletizer not keeping pace with upstream production.
probable_causes: Palletizer cycle time slower than production rate
Frequent faults/stops on palletizer reducing throughput
Pattern change in progress — slower cycle during changeover
Pallet dispenser not keeping up with demand
Discharge conveyor full — completed pallets not being removed
fix_steps: Check palletizer status — clear any active faults
Verify discharge side — are completed pallets being removed by forklift?
If changeover, allow extra time for pattern setup to complete
Check pallet magazine — refill if low or empty
Review production rate vs palletizer rated speed — may need to slow production
Check for recurring faults that keep stopping the palletizer
faults.field_trick: Before calling maintenance, check if completed pallets are being removed from the discharge. 80% of 'palletizer too slow' complaints are because nobody is taking away full pallets.

faults.name: VFD Not Ready
faults.description: Drive reports 'Not Ready' status. Indicates a condition preventing the drive from accepting run commands.
probable_causes: STO (Safe Torque Off) input not energized
Drive in local mode — not accepting remote commands
Hardware interlock preventing drive start
Pre-charge sequence not complete — capacitors still charging
Drive configuration fault — missing required parameters
fix_steps: Check STO terminals — 24V must be present
Verify drive is in correct control mode (Remote/Network)
Check hardware interlock wiring — enable input
Wait for pre-charge to complete (drive LED pattern)
Review drive ready conditions in parameter list
faults.field_trick: Check the drive keypad display. Most VFDs show exactly WHY they're not ready. It's almost always STO, mode switch, or enable input.

faults.name: VFD DC Bus Ripple Fault
faults.description: Excessive DC bus voltage ripple detected. Input rectifier or bus capacitors may be degrading.
probable_causes: Input phase lost — single-phasing causes excessive ripple
Bus capacitors losing capacitance — aging electrolytic caps
Input diode bridge rectifier has an open diode
Power supply harmonic interference
fix_steps: Check all 3 input phases with meter — voltage should be balanced within 3%
Measure DC bus voltage — should be stable ~1.35x line voltage, not pulsing
Check bus capacitor ESR if test equipment available
Test input diodes with diode check — all 6 diodes should show ~0.5V drop
If bus caps swollen or leaking, drive needs repair
faults.field_trick: DC bus should be FLAT. If your scope shows 360Hz ripple, you've lost a capacitor. If 120Hz ripple, you've lost an input phase or diode.

faults.name: CompactLogix USB Port Fault
faults.description: CompactLogix processor USB port not responding or causing communication errors during programming.
probable_causes: USB cable too long or poor quality
USB driver issue on programming laptop
USB port physically damaged — bent pins
ESD damage to USB interface
Firmware bug — certain versions have USB issues
fix_steps: Try a different, shorter USB cable — max 3 meters
Update RSLinx Classic USB driver on laptop
Inspect USB port for bent or damaged pins
If USB fails, use Ethernet for programming instead (more reliable)
Update processor firmware if known USB bug exists for current version
faults.field_trick: If USB keeps disconnecting during download, switch to Ethernet. USB is convenient but unreliable for large program transfers. Ethernet is rock solid.

faults.name: PowerFlex 525 F29 - Analog Input Signal Loss
faults.description: PowerFlex 525 lost the 4-20mA or 0-10V analog speed reference signal below configured minimum.
probable_causes: Analog signal wire broken or pulled from terminal
Source device (PLC analog output, potentiometer) failed
Terminal block corrosion causing high-resistance connection
Wrong input type selected — 0-10V source but drive configured for 4-20mA
Shielded cable shield broken — noise corrupting signal
fix_steps: Check signal at drive terminals with multimeter — should be above minimum
Verify source device is outputting signal correctly
Clean and tighten terminal connections
Verify drive analog input configuration matches source type (A401)
Check shield connection — ground at drive end only
Configure fault action: coast, last speed, or preset speed (A543)
faults.field_trick: A 4-20mA signal at rest should read 4mA, not 0mA. If you read 0mA, the wire is broken. If you read 2-3mA, there's a bad connection somewhere.

faults.name: Conveyor Belt Rip Detection
faults.description: Belt rip detection system activated. Sensor detected longitudinal tear in conveyor belt that could lead to catastrophic failure.
probable_causes: Sharp foreign object punctured belt and tore along length
Belt splice failed and edge is catching
Material trapped between belt and frame cutting into belt
Previous repair patch failed and is catching on structure
fix_steps: STOP CONVEYOR IMMEDIATELY — belt rip can cause complete belt destruction
Inspect belt for tear location and extent
Remove any foreign objects embedded in belt
Temporary repair with belt clamps if tear is small (<12 inches)
Schedule vulcanized repair or belt replacement for any significant tear
Investigate source of foreign object to prevent recurrence
faults.field_trick: A belt rip that's caught early (1-2 feet) can be repaired. A belt rip that runs (50+ feet) means a new belt. Stop the conveyor the instant rip detection triggers — don't 'run it to the end of the shift'.

faults.name: Conveyor Take-up at Maximum
faults.description: Belt take-up mechanism has reached maximum travel. Belt tension is at the adjustment limit.
probable_causes: Belt has stretched to maximum allowable elongation — needs replacement
Belt splice shortened belt length during last repair
Take-up weight insufficient — belt slipping despite full take-up travel
Temperature cycling causing belt to stretch beyond adjustment
fix_steps: Measure belt elongation — if >2% of original length, schedule replacement
If belt was shortened for splice repair, consider adding belt length
Check take-up mechanism — gravity, screw, or hydraulic type
Verify take-up is not binding on frame — must travel freely
Order replacement belt if elongation is beyond repair
faults.field_trick: Mark the take-up carriage position monthly. If it moves more than 1 inch per month, the belt is stretching and you've got 3-6 months before it needs replacement.

faults.name: AS/RS End-of-Aisle Transfer Fault
faults.description: End-of-aisle pallet transfer conveyor or turntable reported fault. Pallets cannot enter or exit the AS/RS crane aisle.
probable_causes: Transfer conveyor motor fault — VFD or motor issue
Turntable position sensor not confirming alignment
Pallet not centered on transfer — overhang preventing handoff
Communication fault between transfer controller and crane controller
Safety interlock preventing transfer — guard door open
fix_steps: Check transfer equipment status panel — identify specific fault
Verify turntable is in correct position — check alignment sensors
Check pallet position — must be centered within tolerance
Verify communication between transfer and crane controllers
Check safety interlocks — all guards must be closed
Run transfer in manual mode to verify mechanical operation
faults.field_trick: End-of-aisle transfers are the #1 bottleneck in any AS/RS. If the transfer is down, the whole crane is down. Keep spare VFDs and sensors at the transfer station.

faults.name: Inkjet Printer Head Fault
faults.description: Inkjet date/lot code printer reports print head fault. Codes may be missing, illegible, or not printing.
probable_causes: Print head clogged — ink dried in nozzles
Ink supply empty or low
Print head to product distance incorrect — moved during operation
Ink type incompatible with product surface
Encoder signal lost — printer doesn't know when to fire
fix_steps: Run print head cleaning cycle — most printers have auto-clean function
Check ink supply — refill if low
Verify print head distance to product — typically 5-15mm per spec
If dried ink, soak print head in manufacturer-recommended solvent
Check product encoder signal — verify pulse count on test print
Replace print head if cleaning doesn't restore print quality
faults.field_trick: If the printer works when you clean it but clogs again in an hour, the ink is bad or the environment is too dry. Check ink expiry date and add humidity control.

faults.name: Cartoner Carton Size Mismatch
faults.description: Cartoning machine detected wrong carton size loaded for current product recipe. Cartons don't match production order.
probable_causes: Wrong carton blank loaded in magazine — operator error
Recipe not changed when switching products
Carton size sensor malfunctioning — reading wrong dimensions
Mixed carton sizes in magazine — leftover from previous run
fix_steps: Verify carton blanks match current production order specification
Select correct recipe on HMI for current product
Remove any mixed cartons from magazine
Recalibrate carton size sensor if readings are off
Implement carton verification at magazine loading point
Run test carton through machine to verify correct size
faults.field_trick: Before starting a new product run, always pull one carton blank and hand-fold it. If the product doesn't fit the carton, wrong blanks are loaded. Catches the mistake before you make 500 bad ones.

faults.name: Electrical Panel Overtemperature
faults.description: Electrical panel internal temperature exceeds alarm threshold. Risk of component failure or fire.
probable_causes: Cabinet cooling fan or A/C unit failure
Excessive heat load — too many drives or components in panel
Panel door left open — hot ambient air entering
Air filter clogged — blocking cooling airflow
Loose bus connections generating heat from high-resistance joints
fix_steps: Check cabinet cooling system — A/C unit, fans, heat exchangers
Clean or replace air filters on panel ventilation system
Verify panel doors are fully closed and sealed
Scan panel interior with IR camera — look for hot spots at connections
Tighten all bus connections — use calibrated torque wrench
If chronic, add supplemental cooling or reduce heat load
faults.field_trick: An IR scan of your panel connections once a year catches loose connections before they start fires. The ones that burn panels are the ones nobody checked.

faults.name: Tank Level Sensor Fault
faults.description: Process tank level sensor reading is erratic, stuck, or has lost signal. Tank level cannot be monitored accurately.
probable_causes: Ultrasonic sensor face coated with product splatter
Radar sensor antenna buildup affecting signal
Float switch stuck in one position — corrosion or mechanical jam
Capacitance probe coated — needs cleaning
4-20mA signal wire fault — sensor OK but signal not reaching PLC
fix_steps: Clean sensor face/antenna — remove product buildup
Check sensor reading vs visual level check — compare actual to displayed
Verify 4-20mA signal at PLC input — should track with level changes
If float switch, manually actuate to free stuck mechanism
For capacitance probes, remove and clean the probe rod
Replace sensor if cleaning doesn't restore accurate reading
faults.field_trick: For ultrasonic level sensors, angle the sensor 2-3 degrees off vertical. This prevents false echoes from the liquid surface directly below and gives a cleaner signal.

faults.name: SCADA Server Communication Lost
faults.description: SCADA or DCS server lost communication with field devices. Operator screens showing stale data or communication errors.
probable_causes: Network infrastructure failure — switch, router, or fiber optic link
SCADA server application crashed or froze
Database server overloaded — historical data queries blocking real-time
Firewall rule change blocking OPC or Modbus traffic
Server hardware fault — NIC, memory, or storage failure
fix_steps: Check SCADA server status — application running, CPU and memory usage
Verify network connectivity — ping PLCs from SCADA server
Check network switches and routers in the path
Review Windows Event Log for errors on SCADA server
Restart SCADA application if hung — do NOT restart server unless necessary
Check OPC server connections — may need manual reconnection
faults.field_trick: Before restarting the SCADA server, try just restarting the OPC or communication service. Full server restart can take 15-20 minutes. Service restart is usually 30 seconds.

faults.name: Two-Hand Control Timing Violation
faults.description: Two-hand safety control detected timing violation. Operator did not press both buttons within required time window.
probable_causes: Operator pressed buttons with only one hand or not simultaneously
One button mechanically stuck — appears pressed but contact not made
Button contact block failed — electrical contact not closing
Timer relay malfunction — timing window too narrow
Wiring fault causing one channel delay
fix_steps: Verify both buttons operate freely — no mechanical binding
Test each button contact block individually with meter
Check timer relay setting — typically 0.5 second window for two-hand activation
Verify wiring — no excessive resistance on either channel
Replace button contact blocks if contacts degraded
Retrain operator on proper two-hand control technique
faults.field_trick: Two-hand controls must be pressed within 0.5 seconds of each other. If the operator is having trouble, check the buttons — a sticky button adds just enough delay to violate timing.

faults.name: Conveyor Weighing Scale Overload
faults.description: In-motion conveyor scale reports weight exceeding maximum capacity. Scale accuracy compromised.
probable_causes: Product weight exceeds scale rated capacity
Multiple products on scale platform simultaneously
Scale zeroed incorrectly — tare weight wrong
Debris on scale platform adding weight
Load cell mechanical overload — exceeded max rated capacity
fix_steps: Verify product weight vs scale capacity rating
Ensure only one product at a time on scale platform — adjust conveyor spacing
Re-zero/tare the scale with platform empty and clean
Clean scale platform — remove any debris or product buildup
Check load cell output — may be permanently damaged if severely overloaded
Inspect load cell for physical deformation — replace if damaged
faults.field_trick: If the scale suddenly reads heavy on everything, check under the platform. Product crumbs and debris build up under the scale and add false weight. A 5-minute cleaning fixes it.

faults.name: PowerFlex 755 F100+ Configurable Fault
faults.description: User-configurable fault on PowerFlex 755 triggered. These are custom faults set up by the integrator for application-specific conditions.
probable_causes: Application-specific condition met (configured by integrator)
Process variable exceeded user-set threshold
Digital input configured as fault input is active
Analog input reading out of user-configured acceptable range
Custom fault logic in PLC triggered via network command
fix_steps: Check fault code number — F100+ faults are site-specific
Refer to site-specific drive documentation for fault definitions
Check condition that triggered the custom fault — may be process-related
Review drive parameter group for the specific fault (usually P100+)
Contact site integrator or controls engineer for application-specific support
faults.field_trick: F100+ on a PF755 is a CUSTOM fault — not in the Rockwell manual. Look at the panel door — there should be a site-specific fault list. If not, check the PLC code for what triggers it.

faults.name: HVAC Supply Air Temperature Alarm
faults.description: Plant HVAC system supply air temperature outside acceptable range for production area.
probable_causes: Chiller or boiler serving AHU is down
Cooling or heating coil valve actuator failed — stuck open or closed
Mixed air dampers not modulating — stuck in one position
Temperature sensor in duct drifted or failed
Filter clogged — restricting airflow and affecting temperature
fix_steps: Check AHU (Air Handling Unit) status — operating normally?
Verify chiller/boiler is running and supplying correct temperature medium
Check coil valve position — should modulate with demand
Test temperature sensor — compare to portable probe reading
Replace air filters if differential pressure indicates clogged
Check damper positions and actuator operation
faults.field_trick: If the supply air temp is wrong but everything 'looks' fine, check the mixing dampers. A stuck return air damper or outside air damper completely changes the supply temp.

faults.name: VFD Overcurrent — Deceleration
faults.description: Drive tripped on overcurrent during deceleration ramp. Regenerative energy from the load exceeded the DC bus capacity.
probable_causes: Deceleration time too short — high-inertia load pumps energy back into DC bus faster than it can absorb
No dynamic braking resistor installed on a high-inertia application
Braking resistor open or wiring disconnected — check terminals DB+ and DB-
VFD DC bus capacitors degraded — cannot absorb regenerative energy
Load is overhauling — gravity or process force driving motor faster than commanded speed
fix_steps: Increase deceleration time by 50-100% and retest
If application requires fast stopping, verify dynamic braking resistor is installed and connected to DB+/DB- terminals
Measure braking resistor with ohmmeter — compare to rated value on resistor nameplate
Check DC bus voltage during decel using VFD keypad monitor — should not exceed rated bus voltage (typically 800VDC for 480V drive)
Enable coast-to-stop if application allows — eliminates regen entirely
For overhauling loads (downhill conveyors, hoists), install or size up the braking resistor
faults.field_trick: If you don't have a braking resistor and need a temporary fix, set decel time to 999 seconds and enable coast-to-stop. The motor will coast down naturally. Not a permanent fix but gets production running.

faults.name: VFD Overcurrent — Steady State
faults.description: Drive tripped on overcurrent while running at constant speed. Current exceeded trip threshold during normal operation, not during accel or decel.
probable_causes: Mechanical load increased suddenly — product pileup, bearing failure, or process change
Motor winding insulation degrading — intermittent phase-to-phase short under load
VFD output cable damaged — insulation breakdown in conduit, especially at sharp bends or pull points
Ambient temperature too high — VFD derating due to enclosure temperature above 40C/104F
IGBT module degrading — one leg conducting more than others
fix_steps: Monitor output current on VFD display during operation — compare to motor FLA
Check motor temperature with IR thermometer — compare all three phases at motor junction box
Megger VFD output cable from drive terminals to motor — disconnect both ends first
Check enclosure ambient temperature — install ventilation fan if above 40C
Compare all three output phase currents with clamp meter — more than 5% imbalance indicates motor or cable issue
If current is normal but fault persists, VFD current sensing board or CT may be faulty
faults.field_trick: Clamp all three motor leads through a single CT window — if the reading is not zero, you have a ground fault. On a healthy motor with balanced phases, the vector sum of all three phases is zero.

faults.name: VFD Motor Overload
faults.description: VFD electronic motor overload protection tripped. Motor current exceeded the programmed overload threshold for the programmed time.
probable_causes: Mechanical load increased — bearing failure, product buildup, or process change
Motor overload parameter set too low — should be set to motor nameplate FLA
Ambient temperature at motor above rated — motor service factor consumed
Motor cooling fan broken or blocked — TEFC fan shroud clogged
Running motor at low speed for extended time — reduced cooling at low RPM on self-cooled motors
fix_steps: Check motor nameplate FLA and verify VFD motor overload parameter matches
Measure actual motor current with clamp meter — compare to FLA
Check motor surface temperature with IR gun — should not exceed nameplate insulation class rating
Inspect motor fan and fan shroud — clear debris, verify fan spins freely
If running continuously below 30Hz on a TEFC motor, add a forced-cooling blower kit
Check mechanical load — rotate shaft by hand with motor disconnected, feel for binding
faults.field_trick: If a motor runs fine for 2 hours then trips on overload, check the motor fan shroud first. A clogged shroud lets the motor slowly heat up until the thermal model trips. Cleaning the shroud takes 5 minutes and is the fix 40% of the time.

faults.name: VFD DC Bus Fault
faults.description: DC bus charging circuit or bus capacitor bank fault detected. Pre-charge resistor or contactor malfunction.
probable_causes: Pre-charge resistor open — resistor burned out from excessive power cycling
DC bus contactor not pulling in — coil failed or aux contact feedback not received by control board
DC bus capacitors degraded — high ESR causing excessive ripple
Bus voltage sensing circuit fault on control board
Excessive power cycling causing pre-charge resistor thermal fatigue
fix_steps: CAUTION: DC bus capacitors store lethal voltage — wait 5 minutes after power off, then verify with meter before touching anything inside drive
Listen for DC bus contactor click when power is applied — should hear relay pull in after 1-2 seconds
Check pre-charge resistor with ohmmeter — compare to marked value (typically 10-100 ohm)
Measure DC bus voltage — should charge to line voltage x 1.414 within 5 seconds of power-up
If bus voltage is unstable or shows excessive ripple, capacitors need replacement — this is a factory repair
Replace pre-charge resistor if open — use exact replacement rating
faults.field_trick: If a VFD keeps blowing pre-charge resistors, someone is cycling power too fast. Pre-charge resistors need 60 seconds between power cycles to cool. Put a time-delay relay in the control circuit to enforce a 60-second minimum off-time.

faults.name: VFD Communication Loss — Modbus RTU
faults.description: VFD lost serial communication with the PLC or HMI via Modbus RTU (RS-485). Drive entered fault or last-speed-hold state.
probable_causes: RS-485 cable disconnected or damaged — check terminal block connections
Baud rate mismatch between VFD and PLC/HMI — both must match exactly
Modbus address conflict — two devices with same slave address on bus
Missing or incorrect termination resistor on RS-485 bus — 120 ohm at each end
PLC communication module faulted or port disabled
RS-485 cable run too long without repeater — max 1200m/4000ft for RS-485
fix_steps: Verify RS-485 wiring: A(+) to A(+), B(-) to B(-), shield grounded at one end only
Confirm baud rate, parity, stop bits, and slave address match on both ends
Check for termination resistors — 120 ohm between A and B at first and last device on bus
Use PLC Modbus diagnostic counters to check for CRC errors, timeouts, and retries
If multiple devices on bus, disconnect all except VFD and test — add back one at a time
Check comm loss action parameter on VFD — set to fault, coast, or hold last speed based on application safety
faults.field_trick: RS-485 polarity is backwards on half the devices out there. If you can't get comms, swap A and B wires at one end. There's no standard — what Rockwell calls A(+), Siemens calls B(+). Just try both.

faults.name: VFD Communication Loss — EtherNet/IP
faults.description: VFD lost EtherNet/IP connection to the PLC controller. CIP connection timed out. Drive entered configured comm loss action state.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable disconnected or damaged — check RJ45 connectors and cable
IP address conflict on the network — another device has same IP
Managed switch port configuration issue — VLAN, speed, or duplex mismatch
PLC controller faulted or rebooted — all CIP connections dropped
Network bandwidth saturated — too many devices or excessive broadcast traffic
VFD Ethernet adapter module firmware incompatible with PLC firmware version
fix_steps: Check VFD network status LEDs — Link LED should be solid green, Activity LED should be flashing
Ping VFD IP address from engineering laptop — confirm basic connectivity
In PLC I/O tree, check VFD module status — look for connection faulted or RPI timeout
Verify IP address, subnet mask, and gateway on VFD match network plan
Check Ethernet cable with cable tester — replace if any pairs fail
Try a different switch port — eliminates switch port as the issue
Check comm loss action: Parameter for action on comm loss (fault/stop/hold last speed)
faults.field_trick: Carry a small unmanaged 5-port switch in your toolbag. If you suspect the plant switch, plug the PLC and VFD directly into your switch. If comms work, the problem is the plant network infrastructure, not the devices.

faults.name: VFD Stall Detection — Motor Not Rotating
faults.description: VFD detected that the motor is not rotating despite output being commanded. Current is flowing but no speed feedback confirmed.
probable_causes: Motor shaft mechanically locked — seized bearing, jammed coupling, or frozen load
VFD output contactor (if used) not closed — motor not connected to drive output
Motor winding open on one phase — single-phasing, motor hums but won't spin
Coupling between motor and load sheared — motor spins but load doesn't move
VFD boost voltage too low for breakaway torque — motor can't overcome static friction
fix_steps: Disconnect motor from load and try to run motor alone — confirms motor vs load issue
Check motor rotation by hand — should spin freely without grinding
Measure resistance on all three motor windings T1-T2, T2-T3, T1-T3 — should be equal within 5%
If output contactor is used, verify it pulls in before VFD run command
Increase VFD start boost/torque boost parameter if motor can't break away from standstill
Check coupling between motor and load — inspect keyway, set screws, and coupling spider
faults.field_trick: A motor that hums loudly but won't spin is almost always a single-phase condition — one winding is open. Check all three lead connections in the motor junction box. A loose wire nut on one lead causes this 90% of the time.

faults.name: VFD Speed Deviation — Feedback Mismatch
faults.description: Actual motor speed deviates from commanded speed beyond the acceptable tolerance window. Encoder or tachometer feedback does not match VFD output frequency.
probable_causes: Encoder coupling slipping on motor shaft — set screw loose
Encoder cable noise pickup — unshielded cable routed near power cables
Encoder failing — intermittent signal dropouts
Motor slipping excessively — worn rotor bars on induction motor
VFD speed deviation tolerance set too tight for the application
fix_steps: Check encoder mounting — verify coupling is tight on shaft with no play
Inspect encoder cable routing — must be separated from power cables by at least 12 inches
Monitor encoder feedback on VFD display vs. commanded speed — note the deviation amount
Check encoder cable shield — must be grounded at the VFD end only
Widen speed deviation tolerance parameter if application allows
Replace encoder if intermittent — test with a known-good encoder first
faults.field_trick: Spin the encoder by hand and watch the VFD speed display. It should increment smoothly. If it jumps erratically or shows negative speed, the encoder is bad. You don't need an oscilloscope for this — the VFD display tells you everything.

faults.name: VFD IGBT / Power Module Fault
faults.description: VFD internal power semiconductor (IGBT) module fault detected. Drive detected a short circuit or desaturation condition in the output power stage.
probable_causes: IGBT module failed — semiconductor end of life or thermal stress damage
Output short circuit — phase-to-phase or phase-to-ground at motor terminals or in cable
Gate driver board failure — IGBT gate signal not reaching power module correctly
Snubber capacitor failed — voltage spike damaged IGBT
Lightning or power surge damaged power stage components
fix_steps: CAUTION: Disconnect all power and wait 5 minutes for DC bus to discharge before opening drive
