Disconnect motor cables from VFD output T1/T2/T3

With multimeter on diode check, test each IGBT: positive lead to DC+, negative to T1, T2, T3 (should show ~0.4-0.7V). Reverse leads (should show OL). Then positive to T1/T2/T3, negative to DC- (should show ~0.4-0.7V)
Any reading of 0 ohm or short indicates a failed IGBT
This is typically a factory or authorized service center repair
Check root cause (output short, power surge) before installing replacement drive
faults.field_trick: You can check IGBTs in the field with a standard multimeter on diode check. Each IGBT should read like two diodes in series. If any read zero in both directions, that module is shorted. This 2-minute test tells you if the drive is repairable or scrap.

faults.name: VFD Analog Input Fault
faults.description: Analog speed reference input (4-20mA or 0-10V) signal is out of range or lost. Drive cannot determine commanded speed.
probable_causes: 4-20mA signal wire broken — current reads 0mA instead of minimum 4mA
Signal source (PLC analog output or potentiometer) powered off or faulted
Shield wire on analog signal cable broken or grounded at both ends — noise causing erratic readings
Wrong signal type selected — VFD configured for 4-20mA but receiving 0-10V or vice versa
Analog input terminal corroded or loose — high resistance contact
fix_steps: Measure signal at VFD analog input terminals with multimeter — 4-20mA or 0-10V expected
If 0mA, trace wire back to source — check for broken wire or loose terminal
Verify VFD analog input configuration matches source type (mA vs V, unipolar vs bipolar)
Check analog signal cable shield — ground at VFD end only, not at source end
Set VFD signal loss action parameter — fault, hold last speed, or go to preset speed
If using 4-20mA, the live-zero (4mA = 0 speed) provides built-in wire break detection
faults.field_trick: Always use 4-20mA over 0-10V for speed reference. With 4-20mA, 0mA means the wire is broken — you get automatic fault detection for free. With 0-10V, a broken wire looks like 0V which means 'run at zero speed' — you'll never know it's broken.

faults.name: VFD External Thermistor Trip — Motor PTC
faults.description: VFD thermistor input detected motor over-temperature via embedded PTC or PT100 sensor in motor windings.
probable_causes: Motor genuinely overheated — overloaded, poor ventilation, or high ambient
PTC thermistor wiring open — broken wire reads as high resistance (hot) to VFD
Motor cooling fan failed — TEFC fan shroud clogged or fan broken
Motor running at low speed too long — insufficient self-cooling below 30Hz
PTC thermistor in motor is degraded — resistance drifted with age
fix_steps: Check motor surface temperature with IR gun — verify it is actually hot
If motor is cool, check PTC wiring from motor to VFD thermistor input — look for broken wire or loose terminal
Measure PTC resistance at VFD terminals — should be <1.5K ohm when motor is cool, >3.3K ohm when hot (typical PTC trip point)
Inspect motor fan and fan shroud — clean and verify fan operation
If motor runs at low speeds continuously, install external forced cooling blower
Replace PTC sensor only if resistance is erratic when motor is at known temperature
faults.field_trick: PTC thermistors in motors fail open more often than motors actually overheat. Before blaming the motor, jumper the PTC input at the VFD with a 1K ohm resistor. If the fault clears and the motor runs cool, the PTC or its wiring is the problem, not the motor.

faults.name: VFD Output Contactor Feedback Fault
faults.description: VFD expected to see output contactor auxiliary contact feedback but did not. Drive will not run if contactor is not confirmed closed.
probable_causes: Output contactor coil failed — not pulling in
Contactor auxiliary contact (aux block) failed or wiring disconnected
Control voltage to contactor coil missing — fuse blown or interlock open
Contactor contacts welded shut — aux feedback shows closed when main contacts are actually open (dangerous)
Wiring error — aux contact wired NO instead of NC or vice versa
fix_steps: Check contactor — command it ON from PLC and verify physical pull-in (listen for click, check indicator)
Verify aux contact status matches main contact state — when contactor pulls in, aux should change state
Check contactor coil voltage — measure at coil terminals when energized
If contactor pulls in but aux contact doesn't change, replace aux contact block
Verify wiring matches VFD manual — some require NO feedback, others NC
Check for welded contacts with contactor de-energized — main contacts should be open, verify with ohmmeter
faults.field_trick: When output contactors start buzzing or chattering, replace them. That buzzing means the contacts are bouncing, which creates arc damage on every bounce. A chattering contactor will weld shut within a week. Don't wait for the failure.

faults.name: VFD Auto-Restart Fault — Retries Exhausted
faults.description: VFD attempted to auto-restart after a fault the configured number of times and all attempts failed. Drive has locked out and requires manual reset.
probable_causes: Underlying fault is persistent — the condition that caused the original trip has not cleared
Intermittent fault occurring frequently — ground fault, power sag, or overload
Auto-restart delay too short — not enough time for condition to clear between attempts
Motor or cable insulation degrading — each restart attempt further stresses failing insulation
Mechanical issue on load side recurring — product jam reforms after each restart
fix_steps: Check VFD fault log — identify what fault code triggered each restart attempt
Address the root cause fault before resetting the auto-restart lockout
Increase time between restart attempts to allow more settling time
If ground fault, do not keep restarting — megger the motor and cable before clearing
Clear the lockout by cycling VFD power or using the fault reset input
Consider disabling auto-restart if the fault is potentially damaging (ground fault, overcurrent)
faults.field_trick: Auto-restart on a ground fault drive will kill the motor. Every restart attempt pushes current through the failing insulation, making it worse. If you see ground faults in the fault log, disable auto-restart immediately and fix the insulation issue.

faults.name: VFD Dynamic Braking Fault
faults.description: VFD detected a fault in the dynamic braking circuit. Braking transistor, braking resistor, or braking resistor thermal switch tripped.
probable_causes: Braking resistor thermal overload — resistor too small for braking duty cycle
Braking resistor open — element burned out from excessive use
Braking resistor thermal switch (if equipped) tripped — needs manual reset
Braking transistor failed — IGBT in braking circuit shorted or open
Wiring between VFD DB terminals and braking resistor loose or disconnected
fix_steps: Check braking resistor temperature — if too hot to touch, duty cycle exceeds resistor rating
Measure braking resistor with ohmmeter at DB+/DB- terminals — compare to rated value
Check for thermal switch on braking resistor — some have manual reset buttons on the resistor housing
Verify wiring at VFD DB+ and DB- terminals — torque connections
If resistor is undersized, install larger resistor or additional resistor in parallel
If braking transistor is failed (internal to VFD), this requires factory repair or drive replacement
faults.field_trick: If the braking resistor is mounted inside the electrical panel, it is probably overheating the panel too. Mount braking resistors outside the enclosure whenever possible. A $50 resistor mounted inside can bake a $5000 VFD sitting next to it.

faults.name: VFD Output Relay Fault — Relay Welded or Open
faults.description: VFD programmable output relay is stuck closed (welded) or not operating. Relay used for fault indication, run status, or interlock feedback.
probable_causes: Relay contacts welded from switching inductive load without suppression — contactor coil, solenoid, etc.
Relay exceeded rated contact current — load current too high for relay rating (typically 2A)
Relay mechanical wear — exceeded cycle life
Relay function programmed incorrectly — outputs unexpected state
Relay coil burned out — internal to VFD, may require board-level repair
fix_steps: Check relay state with multimeter at relay output terminals — NO/NC/COM
Verify relay programming in VFD parameters — confirm assigned function
If relay is welded, you can hear/feel it not clicking when state changes — listen carefully
Add a snubber (RC network) across the relay contacts if switching inductive loads
If relay is built into VFD, use an external interposing relay driven by VFD digital output to isolate the load
Replace VFD relay board or use external relay as workaround
faults.field_trick: VFD onboard relays are rated for about 100,000 operations at 2A. If your relay switches a contactor coil every 30 seconds, that's 2,880 operations per day — the relay lasts about 35 days. Use an external relay rated for higher current and cycle count.

faults.name: VFD Keypad / Display Fault
faults.description: VFD keypad display is blank, garbled, or non-responsive. Drive may still be running but cannot be monitored or programmed from the keypad.
probable_causes: Keypad ribbon cable or connector loose — vibration can work connectors free over time
Keypad LCD failed — display elements dead but drive still functional
Keypad communication port on main control board failed
Keypad not compatible with this drive model or firmware version
Condensation or contamination on keypad circuit board
fix_steps: Remove and reseat keypad connector — many VFD keypads are detachable
Try a known-good keypad from another identical drive model
Check if drive is still running by measuring output voltage at T1/T2/T3
If keypad is dead but drive runs, use commissioning software (DriveExplorer, PowerFlex tools) via USB or Ethernet as temporary interface
Clean keypad connector pins with isopropyl alcohol and lint-free cloth
Order replacement keypad — ensure correct part number for drive model and firmware version
faults.field_trick: Keep a spare keypad in your tool bag for the most common VFD brand in your plant. A dead keypad doesn't mean a dead drive — the drive is probably still running fine, you just can't see it. Plug in your spare, read the parameters, and assess from there.

faults.name: VFD PID Process Fault — Setpoint Not Reached
faults.description: VFD internal PID controller cannot reach the process setpoint. Feedback signal is not converging to the setpoint within the configured deviation band and time.
probable_causes: Process sensor (pressure, flow, temperature) failed or miscalibrated — VFD seeing wrong feedback
Process demand exceeds motor/pump capacity — motor at 100% speed but can't meet setpoint
PID tuning too aggressive or too sluggish — oscillating or not responding
Feedback signal wiring issue — intermittent or noisy analog signal
System leak or bypass valve open — process variable drains away as fast as pump/fan adds
fix_steps: Compare VFD PID feedback value to an independent measurement (field gauge, handheld) — verify sensor accuracy
Check if motor is already at max speed — if at 60Hz and setpoint not reached, the system is undersized
Check PID tuning parameters: P (proportional), I (integral), D (derivative) — start with P=1.0, I=5.0s, D=0
Inspect feedback sensor wiring — check for noise, loose connections, corroded terminals
Look for process leaks, open bypass valves, or other reasons the process variable can't be maintained
Widen the PID deviation alarm band if the process has normal fluctuations
faults.field_trick: Put the VFD in manual speed mode (not PID) and slowly ramp speed up while watching the process variable. If the process responds predictably, the problem is PID tuning. If the process doesn't respond at all, the problem is mechanical — check valves, dampers, coupling.

faults.name: VFD Firmware / Software Fault
faults.description: VFD internal firmware error or watchdog timer fault. Control processor detected an internal software exception.
probable_causes: Firmware corruption — power loss during firmware update
Control board hardware fault — processor or memory failure
EMI/RFI interference causing processor upset — unshielded control wiring near high-power cables
Firmware bug triggered by specific parameter combination or operating condition
Static discharge to control terminals during maintenance
fix_steps: Cycle power to VFD — many firmware faults are transient and clear on power cycle
If fault recurs immediately after power-up, note the exact fault code for manufacturer tech support
Try firmware reflash using manufacturer software tool (USB or serial)
Ensure all control wiring is shielded and separated from power wiring
If firmware reflash fails, control board needs replacement
Before replacing board, backup all parameters — new board will have factory defaults
faults.field_trick: Keep the latest firmware file for your plant's VFD models on a USB drive in your toolbox. A corrupted drive that won't boot can often be recovered by reflashing firmware through the USB port or serial bootloader. This saves a 2-week lead time on a new control board.

faults.name: ControlLogix 1756-EN2T Connection Timeout — I/O Module
faults.description: A 1756-EN2T/EN2TR EtherNet/IP module lost connection to a remote I/O rack, VFD, or third-party device. CIP connection timed out at the configured RPI interval.
probable_causes: Remote device powered off or faulted — check remote rack or device status
Ethernet cable damaged or disconnected between managed switch and remote device
Network congestion causing packet delays exceeding RPI timeout
IP address changed on remote device — doesn't match controller configuration
Managed switch port went into error-disable or spanning tree blocked state
fix_steps: In RSLogix 5000 / Studio 5000, check I/O tree — identify which module shows connection faulted (red X)
Ping the faulted device IP from the engineering PC — confirm basic connectivity
Check Ethernet cable and switch port LED — link light should be solid green
Compare device IP address to what is configured in the controller I/O module definition
Check managed switch for port errors — CRC errors, late collisions indicate cable issues
If using DLR ring, check ring status — a break causes topology change and brief connection drop
Increase RPI (requested packet interval) if network is borderline — but note this increases response time
faults.field_trick: When you lose comms to a remote I/O rack, check the EN2T module status LED first — STEADY green is good, FLASHING green means no connections configured, RED means major fault, FLASHING RED means IP conflict. The LED tells you the category of problem before you even open software.

faults.name: CompactLogix 1769 I/O Module Fault
faults.description: A 1769-series I/O module in the CompactLogix local chassis has faulted. Module LED shows solid red or flashing red. I/O data from this module is invalid.
probable_causes: Module not seated properly in backplane — latches not fully engaged
Module firmware incompatible with controller firmware revision
Module configuration in controller doesn't match physical module type
24VDC field-side power supply to module is missing or low — some 1769 modules need separate 24VDC
Module hardware failure — internal fuse blown or circuitry damaged by field wiring short
fix_steps: Check module status LED — solid RED is hardware fault, flashing RED is configuration/comm fault
Remove and reseat the module — ensure both latches click fully into the backplane connector
In Studio 5000, right-click the module in I/O tree and check Module Properties > Connection tab for specific fault text
Verify module catalog number matches what is configured in the controller
Check 24VDC field power to the module — measure at the module power terminals
Try the module in a different slot to rule out backplane slot damage
Flash module firmware to match controller compatibility list if firmware mismatch
faults.field_trick: CompactLogix 1769 modules have tiny internal fuses that blow when field wiring shorts. Before replacing a 'dead' module, check if the field-side fuse blew — on 1769-IQ16 input modules it is a surface-mount fuse on the board. A magnifying glass and soldering iron can save a $300 module.

faults.name: PowerFlex 525 Fault F002 — Auxiliary Input
faults.description: PowerFlex 525 external fault input is active. Digital input configured as 'Auxiliary Fault' is seeing an active signal from an external device (thermistor, pressure switch, flow switch, etc.).
probable_causes: External device genuinely tripped — motor thermistor, bearing RTD, pressure switch, etc.
Digital input wiring disconnected — if configured as NC (normally closed), open wire = fault
External device failed — contact stuck open or closed
Wrong digital input function assignment — input configured as Aux Fault when it should be something else
24VDC supply to external device circuit lost
fix_steps: Identify which digital input is configured as Aux Fault — check P033 [Digital In1 Sel] through P038
Trace wiring from that digital input terminal back to the external device
Check external device status — is the thermal switch tripped, pressure switch open, etc.
Measure voltage at the digital input terminal — 24VDC present or absent tells you the circuit state
If external device is healthy, check wiring continuity end-to-end
If you need to bypass temporarily for troubleshooting, change the digital input function to 'Not Used' — but restore before production
faults.field_trick: On a PowerFlex 525, the Aux Fault input is often wired to a motor thermistor relay. If the motor is cool and the fault is active, check the thermistor relay itself — the relay contact may have failed. Bypass the relay temporarily (not the thermistor) to confirm.

faults.name: PowerFlex 525 Fault F041 — Heatsink Overtemperature
faults.description: PowerFlex 525 internal heatsink temperature has exceeded the safe operating limit. Drive shut down to protect power components.
probable_causes: Enclosure ambient temperature too high — ventilation fans failed or filter clogged
VFD fan assembly failed — the 525 has a replaceable fan kit on top of the unit
Drive mounted too close to other heat sources — insufficient spacing between drives
Drive is overloaded — running above continuous duty rating for extended period
Heatsink compound between power module and heatsink has dried out (rare, old drives)
fix_steps: Check VFD fan operation — remove fan from top of PF525 and verify it spins freely and runs when powered
Order replacement fan kit (Rockwell catalog 25-FAN-525) if fan is dead
Measure enclosure ambient at drive inlet — must be below 50C for PF525 without derating
Clean enclosure filter and ensure ventilation fan is running
Check mounting spacing — PF525 requires minimum 25mm (1 inch) side-to-side clearance
Verify drive is not continuously overloaded — check load current vs drive rating
faults.field_trick: PowerFlex 525 fan assemblies are $60 and field-replaceable in 5 minutes — two screws, unplug the connector, swap. Keep one in the storeroom for every 5 drives. When a fan starts buzzing, replace it that day — don't wait for the overheat trip.

faults.name: PowerFlex 755 Network Fault — Adapter Module
faults.description: PowerFlex 755 lost communication with the PLC controller through its network adapter module (20-COMM-E EtherNet/IP or 20-COMM-D DeviceNet). Drive entered comm loss action state.
probable_causes: Network adapter module 20-COMM-E not seated properly in drive slot — vibration walked it loose
EtherNet cable disconnected or damaged at adapter module RJ45 port
IP address conflict on the EtherNet/IP network
PLC controller faulted or was downloaded — CIP connections dropped during download
20-COMM module firmware incompatible with drive firmware — version mismatch
Network adapter module hardware failure — check module status LED
fix_steps: Check 20-COMM module status LEDs — NS (Network Status) should be steady GREEN for connected
Reseat the 20-COMM module in the PowerFlex 755 option slot — push firmly until latch clicks
Ping the adapter module IP address from engineering laptop
In Studio 5000 I/O tree, check connection status of the PF755 module definition
Verify firmware compatibility between 20-COMM-E and PowerFlex 755 main firmware — use Rockwell compatibility matrix
Check comm loss action parameter in drive — set to appropriate action (fault, coast, hold last speed)
Try replacing 20-COMM module with spare if LED shows RED
faults.field_trick: The 20-COMM-E module has two Ethernet ports for DLR ring topology. If you plug into port 1 and get nothing, try port 2 before you call it dead. Also, if you see the NS LED flashing GREEN rapidly, the module is getting power but has no IP — run BOOTP.

faults.name: PowerFlex 755 Fault F12 — HW Overcurrent
faults.description: PowerFlex 755 hardware instantaneous overcurrent trip. Output current exceeded the drive peak current rating in less than one control cycle.
probable_causes: Output short circuit — phase-to-phase or phase-to-ground in cable or motor
IGBT power module failing — shoot-through condition in one leg
Motor insulation failure — winding shorted to ground
Loose output power connection — arcing at terminal lug causing current spike
Cable insulation breakdown — especially at conduit entry points or sharp bends
fix_steps: Do NOT simply reset and try again — this fault indicates a serious overcurrent condition
Disconnect motor leads at drive output terminals U/V/W
Megger motor windings phase-to-phase and phase-to-ground — expect >5 Mohm at 500V test
Megger output cable from drive to motor junction box — test each conductor to ground
With motor disconnected, attempt to run drive — if F12 recurs with no motor, the drive IGBT is failed
Check all output power connections for tightness — use calibrated torque wrench
If motor and cable pass megger, reconnect one phase at a time to isolate the faulted leg
faults.field_trick: On a PowerFlex 755, an F12 at zero speed (before the motor even starts moving) almost always means an IGBT is shorted. An F12 at full speed during load changes usually means motor insulation. The speed at which it trips tells you where to look.

faults.name: PanelView HMI — No Controller Communication
faults.description: PanelView Plus or PanelView 5000 HMI cannot communicate with the ControlLogix or CompactLogix controller. Screens show stale data or communication error indicators.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable between HMI and switch disconnected or damaged
Controller IP address changed — HMI still pointing to old address
Controller in Program mode — some tag connections drop when not in Run mode
HMI application has wrong controller path configured in RSLinx Enterprise or FactoryTalk Linx
Network switch port issue — try a different port
HMI Ethernet adapter set to wrong IP address or subnet
fix_steps: Check HMI communication status — look for red X or broken link icon on screen objects
Press and hold the Windows button on PanelView Plus to access settings — check IP address configuration
Ping the controller IP address from the HMI (if accessible) or from a laptop on the same network
In FactoryTalk View ME, check the Communication Setup — verify shortcut points to correct controller IP
Check Ethernet cable and switch port — verify link LEDs on both HMI and switch
Verify controller is in RUN mode and not faulted
If HMI was recently downloaded, verify the runtime application communication path is correct
faults.field_trick: On a PanelView Plus 7, press and hold the upper-right corner of the screen for 5 seconds to open the FactoryTalk Diagnostics viewer. It will show you the exact communication error message, including which controller path is failing. This is faster than pulling out a laptop.

faults.name: ControlLogix 1756-L7SP Safety Partner Fault
faults.description: The safety partner processor (1756-L7SP) in a GuardLogix system has faulted. Safety task is not executing. Safety I/O connections may be dropped.
probable_causes: Safety partner firmware version does not match primary controller firmware — must be identical
Safety partner hardware failure — module LED shows solid red
Safety task execution exceeded the safety task period — watchdog timeout
Safety partner not properly seated in chassis — backplane connection issue
Corrupted safety application — safety signature mismatch between primary and partner
fix_steps: Check safety partner module status LED — STEADY RED is major fault, FLASHING RED is configuration issue
In Studio 5000, check Controller Properties > Safety tab for specific safety partner fault code
Verify primary and partner firmware versions are identical — update if mismatched
Reseat the safety partner module in the chassis
If safety signature mismatch, you must reload the safety application and re-validate — this requires the safety task password
Check safety task period vs. actual execution time — reduce safety program complexity or increase period if watchdog tripping
Replacing a safety partner requires re-validating the safety application per safety lifecycle requirements
faults.field_trick: After any GuardLogix firmware update, you MUST update both the primary and the safety partner to the same version. They must match exactly. If you update one and not the other, you get a partner fault and the safety system goes down. Always update them as a pair during the same maintenance window.

faults.name: ControlLogix Servo Axis Fault — Motion Group Error
faults.description: A Kinetix servo axis in a ControlLogix motion group has faulted. The motion group has stopped all coordinated axes. Common on Kinetix 5500/5700 servo drives.
probable_causes: Position error exceeded tolerance — axis could not follow commanded profile
Motor feedback (encoder/resolver) signal lost — cable or connector issue
Drive hardware fault — overvoltage, overcurrent, or overtemperature on Kinetix drive
Motor winding fault — phase imbalance or ground fault detected by drive
Mechanical obstruction — axis cannot physically move to commanded position
Motion group update rate too fast for number of axes configured
fix_steps: In Studio 5000, check Axis Properties > Faults tab for the specific axis fault code and timestamp
Check Kinetix drive display for local fault code — cross-reference with Kinetix manual
Inspect motor feedback cable — check connectors at motor and drive end, look for damage
Check for mechanical obstruction — manually move axis (if safe) to verify free travel
Clear the axis fault using MASR (Motion Axis Stop and Reset) instruction or through Axis Properties
If position error, check tuning gains — reduce velocity and acceleration commands
Monitor axis following error during normal operation — trending upward means mechanical wear or tuning drift
faults.field_trick: When a servo axis trips on following error, check the motor feedback cable FIRST. A flaky encoder connection causes intermittent position jumps that look like following errors. Wiggle the feedback cable connector at the motor while watching the position in Studio 5000 — if it jumps, replace the cable.

faults.name: ControlLogix Redundancy Switchover Fault
faults.description: ControlLogix enhanced redundancy system experienced a switchover or detected a fault in the redundancy configuration. The secondary controller may not be synchronized with the primary.
probable_causes: Redundancy cable (1756-RMCxx) disconnected or damaged between primary and secondary chassis
Secondary controller hardware failure — cannot synchronize with primary
Firmware version mismatch between primary and secondary controllers
Network connections not mirrored identically between primary and secondary
Chassis power supply failure on one side of the redundant pair
fix_steps: Check redundancy module LEDs on both chassis — both should show SOLID GREEN when synchronized
Verify redundancy cable (1756-RMCxx) connections at both ends — fiber cables are fragile
In Studio 5000, check Controller Properties > Redundancy tab for sync status and fault details
Verify both controllers have identical firmware versions and identical I/O tree configurations
If switchover occurred, check fault log on both controllers for root cause
Re-synchronize by right-clicking the offline controller and selecting 'Synchronize'
Verify BOTH Ethernet paths are connected — redundancy requires dual network paths
faults.field_trick: ControlLogix redundancy systems MUST have identical firmware, identical I/O trees, and identical network configurations on both sides. If you make any change to the primary (add a module, change a parameter), you must synchronize to the secondary before it takes effect. One missed sync and you're not redundant anymore.

faults.name: EtherNet/IP CIP Error — Connection Rejected (16#0111)
faults.description: A CIP connection request was rejected by the target device with error code 16#0111 (RPI not supported). The requested packet interval is outside the device's supported range.
probable_causes: RPI set too low for the target device — device cannot process data that fast
Device firmware does not support the requested RPI range
Too many connections consuming device bandwidth — each connection adds processing overhead
Multicast vs unicast mismatch — device requires one but configured for the other
Module definition in controller uses wrong electronic keying — revision mismatch
fix_steps: In Studio 5000, right-click the faulted module > Properties > Connection tab to see the specific CIP error code
Increase the RPI value — try 20ms, 50ms, or 100ms depending on application needs
Check device documentation for supported RPI range — some devices minimum is 10ms
Verify electronic keying setting — try 'Compatible Module' instead of 'Exact Match'
Reduce the number of connections to this device — consolidate I/O into fewer connections
Update device firmware if RPI range was expanded in newer firmware
faults.field_trick: When you get CIP errors and can't figure out why, change the electronic keying to 'Disable Keying' temporarily. If the connection comes up, the issue is a catalog number, revision, or firmware mismatch — not a network issue. Then tighten keying back up once you identify the mismatch.

faults.name: EtherNet/IP CIP Error — Module Not Responding (16#0204)
faults.description: CIP connection to target device failed with error 16#0204 (Unconnected Request Timeout). The device was found on the network but is not responding to the CIP connection request.
probable_causes: Device is in bootup sequence — not yet ready to accept CIP connections
Device CIP stack crashed or hung — needs power cycle
Device configured for a different CIP connection type than what controller is requesting
Device has reached maximum concurrent CIP connection limit
Device IP is responding to ping but CIP stack on device is not functional
fix_steps: Power cycle the target device — wait for full boot before retesting
Ping the device IP — confirm Layer 3 connectivity is good
Check if device has a max connections limit — some devices support only 1-4 CIP connections
In Studio 5000, inhibit the module, wait 10 seconds, then uninhibit to force a fresh connection request
Try connecting to the device with RSLinx Enterprise browse — if RSLinx can see it but Studio 5000 can't connect, the device's connection resources may be exhausted
Update device firmware — CIP stack bugs are common in older firmware revisions
faults.field_trick: If a device pings fine but CIP won't connect, try reducing the number of controllers talking to that device. Every PLC that connects to a remote I/O rack uses a CIP connection. Some 1756-EN2T modules max out at 128 connections — in a big plant, you can hit that limit.

faults.name: ControlLogix 1756 Power Supply Fault
faults.description: 1756 chassis power supply module (1756-PA72, 1756-PB72, etc.) has faulted. Backplane power may be lost or degraded, causing all modules in chassis to fault or reboot.
probable_causes: Power supply overloaded — total current draw of all modules exceeds supply rating
Input AC power to supply lost or below voltage threshold — check incoming power
Power supply internal failure — capacitor aging, fan failure (PA75 has internal fan)
Backplane connector on power supply not fully seated — vibration worked it loose
Redundant power supply failed — if using dual supplies (1756-PA75R)
fix_steps: Check power supply LED — GREEN = OK, RED = fault, OFF = no input power
Measure input voltage at power supply terminals — verify within rating (120VAC or 240VAC depending on model)
Calculate total chassis current draw — add up all module current requirements and compare to supply rating
If overloaded, split modules across two chassis or upgrade to higher-capacity supply
Reseat power supply backplane connector — remove and reinstall power supply
If supply has internal fan (1756-PA75), verify fan is running — listen for it
For critical systems, install redundant power supplies (1756-PA75R pair)
faults.field_trick: The 1756-PA72 is rated at 10A on the backplane. If you have a full chassis of I/O modules plus a motion module, you're probably over the limit. Check the Rockwell Power Supply Selection Tool on their website — enter your modules and it tells you exactly how many amps you need.

faults.name: ControlLogix Task Watchdog Timeout
faults.description: A periodic or event task in the ControlLogix controller exceeded its configured watchdog time. The task was aborted. If this is the safety task, safety outputs go to their configured fault state.
probable_causes: Task period set too tight for the amount of logic in the task — scan time exceeds period
Unintentional infinite loop in a user program routine — AOI or subroutine call chain
Too many program files in a single task — excessive overhead
Controller CPU overloaded — tasks competing for processor time
Firmware defect causing one instruction to take longer than expected
fix_steps: In Studio 5000, check Controller Properties > Tasks tab — compare task period to max scan time
Identify which task timed out — check the major fault code for task number
Increase the task watchdog time temporarily to restore operation — but investigate root cause
Use the Controller Properties > Performance tab to view CPU utilization by task
Move non-time-critical routines to a slower continuous task
Check for any recently modified AOIs or programs that might have introduced a long execution path
If safety task, this is a SIL integrity issue — cannot simply increase watchdog without safety review
faults.field_trick: Go to Controller Properties > Tasks and look at the 'Max Scan Time' column. If max scan time is within 80% of the task period, you're one load spike away from a watchdog trip. Best practice is max scan should be below 50% of the period to leave headroom.

faults.name: ControlLogix Memory Full — Cannot Download
faults.description: ControlLogix controller memory is full or nearly full. Cannot download program changes or add new tags. Controller may run but has no room for modifications.
probable_causes: Program is too large for the controller memory capacity
Excessive unused tags and data types consuming memory — program accumulated cruft over years
Large data arrays (UDT arrays, DINT arrays) consuming available memory
Trending or data logging consuming controller memory
Wrong controller model for the application — need higher memory option
fix_steps: In Studio 5000, check Controller Properties > General tab for memory usage percentage
Use the Unused Tags cross-reference to identify and delete tags not referenced by any program
Check for large arrays that may be oversized — reduce array dimensions if elements are unused
Remove or reduce data logging scope if running data log in the controller
Consider upgrading to a higher-memory controller (e.g., 1756-L71 to 1756-L73 or 1756-L83E)
Archive and delete unused routine shells, AOIs, and data types
faults.field_trick: Before deleting anything from a running production controller, SAVE A FULL BACKUP including tag values. Then upload from the running controller to get the latest version. Compare your offline project to the upload to identify differences. Never assume your offline project matches what's running.

faults.name: POINT I/O 1734 Module — Point Fault
faults.description: A specific point (channel) on a 1734 POINT I/O module has faulted. Individual channel reporting a wire-off, over-range, or under-range condition.
probable_causes: Field wire disconnected from the terminal — wire pulled out or terminal screw loose
Analog input sensor failed — transmitter output is 0mA (below 4mA live zero)
Digital input wire broken — module detects open circuit if pulse test is enabled
Analog output driving an open circuit — load disconnected
Module channel damaged by overvoltage or overcurrent from field wiring error
fix_steps: In Studio 5000, check the module's channel fault status — identifies exactly which point has the fault
Check the field wiring at the faulted terminal — tug test all wires gently
For analog inputs, measure the signal at the terminal with a multimeter — 4-20mA should be present
For digital inputs, verify 24VDC is present at the input terminal when the field device is active
Check if the point fault is configured to cause a module fault — can be changed per channel in module properties
If channel is damaged, the entire module must be replaced — individual channels are not field-replaceable
faults.field_trick: 1734 POINT I/O terminal base and module are separate pieces. When troubleshooting, you can swap just the module onto a known-good terminal base to rule out the base. Bases rarely fail, but corroded base contacts from a panel flood will cause random point faults.

faults.name: Stratix Managed Switch — Port Error / Redundancy Fault
faults.description: Allen-Bradley Stratix 5700 or 5400 managed switch detected a port error, ring break, or redundancy protocol fault. Network topology may be degraded.
probable_causes: DLR ring cable broken — single point of failure, ring operates in linear mode
REP ring segment down — switch detects topology change
Port CRC errors from damaged Ethernet cable or bad connector
Port speed/duplex mismatch — auto-negotiate failure with connected device
Switch port went into err-disable from broadcast storm or loop detection
fix_steps: Access Stratix switch web interface — check port status page for error counts and port state
For DLR ring fault, check DLR status LED on switch — identify which ring port lost link
Check Ethernet cables on faulted port — replace if CRC error count is incrementing
If port is err-disabled, identify root cause (loop, broadcast storm) before re-enabling
Verify port speed matches connected device — set both to auto-negotiate or both to fixed 100Mbps full-duplex
For ring redundancy, walk the ring path physically — find the break point using link LEDs at each device
faults.field_trick: When a DLR ring breaks, all devices still communicate because the ring 'opens' into a linear chain. But you now have zero redundancy. The Stratix ring supervisor LED goes from GREEN to RED. Fix the break ASAP — the next cable failure takes the whole network down.

faults.name: PowerFlex 525 Safe Torque Off (STO) Fault
faults.description: PowerFlex 525 STO safety input is de-energized. Drive output is hardware-disabled by the built-in STO function. This is a designed safety feature, not a drive malfunction.
probable_causes: Safety relay or safety PLC de-energized the STO input — intentional safety stop
E-stop activated — STO circuit is in the E-stop chain
Guard door opened — STO wired through guard switch circuit
STO input wiring disconnected — terminal 01 and/or 02 not receiving 24VDC
If STO not used, jumper wires on terminals 01-02 may have fallen off or were never installed
fix_steps: Check 24VDC at STO input terminals 01 (STO1) and 02 (STO2) — both must have 24VDC to enable drive output
If STO is wired through safety relay, check safety relay output status
Verify all E-stops are released and all guard doors are closed
If STO is not being used for safety, install jumper wires: terminal 01 to +24VDC and terminal 02 to +24VDC
STO cannot be reset from the keypad — the hardware input must be satisfied
Check wiring diagram for the STO circuit to identify all devices in the safety chain
faults.field_trick: New PowerFlex 525 drives out of the box will NOT run until you either connect the STO inputs to 24VDC or install the jumper wires. This catches a lot of people during commissioning. Check terminals 01 and 02 — they need 24VDC from a safety-rated source or jumpered.

faults.name: CompactLogix Built-in EtherNet/IP Port Fault
faults.description: CompactLogix 5380 or 5480 controller built-in Ethernet port has lost link or detected a fault. Affects I/O connections, HMI, and any device communicating through this port.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable disconnected or damaged at the controller RJ45 port
Switch port the controller is connected to has failed or been disabled
Duplicate IP address on the network — controller IP conflicts with another device
Controller Ethernet port hardware failure — rare but possible from ESD or lightning
IGMP snooping misconfigured on managed switch — multicast traffic being blocked
fix_steps: Check controller Ethernet port link LED — should be GREEN or AMBER indicating link at 100Mbps or 1Gbps
Try a different Ethernet cable — use a known-good patch cable
Ping the controller from engineering laptop — verify basic IP connectivity
Check for IP conflict using Rockwell BOOTP/DHCP server or 'arp -a' on laptop
If using managed switch, check port configuration — ensure port is not disabled or in wrong VLAN
Check controller web page (browse to controller IP) for Ethernet diagnostics and error counts
If CompactLogix 5380 with dual ports, try the other port to isolate the issue
faults.field_trick: CompactLogix 5380 controllers have two Ethernet ports that can be configured for DLR ring topology. If one port dies, the ring still works through the other. But if you're using both ports for separate networks (not DLR), losing one port means losing one network entirely. Know your topology before troubleshooting.

faults.name: ControlLogix 1756-IF8 / IF16 Analog Input Over-Range
faults.description: A 1756 analog input module is reporting an over-range or under-range condition on one or more channels. Signal is outside the configured measurement range.
probable_causes: 4-20mA transmitter failed high (>20mA) or failed low (<4mA / 0mA)
Thermocouple broken — open junction reads max range on TC input module
Signal wire broken on 4-20mA loop — reads 0mA which is under-range
Incorrect range selected in module configuration — configured for 0-10V but wired for 4-20mA
Field device scaled incorrectly — process variable exceeds transmitter range
fix_steps: In Studio 5000, check the channel status word for the specific over/under-range flag
Measure the signal at the module input terminal with a process meter — verify 4-20mA is present
For TC inputs, check thermocouple integrity — measure resistance across TC leads (should be very low, not open)
Verify module configuration matches wiring — correct signal type (4-20mA, 0-10V, TC type, RTD type)
Check for broken shield wire on analog signal cable — floating shield can couple noise that drives signal out of range
If transmitter is healthy and signal is correct at the module terminal, replace the module channel (module replacement)
faults.field_trick: A broken 4-20mA wire reads 0mA at the module, which is under-range. A broken thermocouple reads maximum temperature — often 999 or 1800 degrees. Know these failure modes so you can instantly tell wire break from sensor failure just by looking at the HMI reading.

faults.name: ControlLogix Firmware Flash Required After Replacement
faults.description: A replacement 1756 module has been installed but its firmware version does not match the project configuration. Module is faulted or inhibited until firmware is updated.
probable_causes: New replacement module shipped with factory default firmware — different from project requirements
Module was pulled from spare stock with old firmware version
Project was developed on a newer firmware and replacement module is an older revision
After ControlFLASH update, module may need a power cycle to complete the update
fix_steps: In Studio 5000, right-click the module > Module Properties > General tab to see required vs. actual firmware revision
Open Rockwell ControlFLASH utility on the engineering PC
Browse to the module through the USB or Ethernet path
Select the target firmware version that matches the project requirement
Flash the module — do NOT interrupt the process (takes 2-10 minutes depending on module)
After flash completes, power cycle the chassis if prompted
Re-download the program if the controller cleared its memory during the process
faults.field_trick: Keep a USB drive with the ControlFLASH firmware files for every module type in your chassis. Download the firmware kit from Rockwell's website once per year. When you need to flash a module at 2 AM, you don't want to be downloading firmware files on a plant floor laptop with spotty WiFi.

faults.name: PowerFlex 525 Parameter Lockout — Security Code Active
faults.description: PowerFlex 525 parameter editing is locked. A security code has been set and must be entered before any parameter changes can be made.
probable_causes: Security code was intentionally set during commissioning — normal security practice
Previous integrator set a code and did not document it
Customer set a code and forgot it
Drive was configured with a security level that locks read access as well as write access
fix_steps: Navigate to parameter P046 (Security Code) on the drive keypad
Enter the correct security code — check commissioning documents, panel drawings, or ask the OEM
If code is unknown, try common defaults: 0000, 1234, 9999, or the last 4 digits of the drive serial number
As a LAST resort: perform a factory reset (hold the Stop/Reset key for 10 seconds while powering up) — this resets ALL parameters including the security code
After factory reset, you must reprogram all parameters from backup — motor, application, and communication settings
Document the security code on the inside of the panel door for future reference
faults.field_trick: If you reset a PowerFlex 525 to factory defaults to clear a forgotten security code, you lose EVERY parameter including the motor data. If you don't have a parameter backup, you need to re-commission the entire drive. ALWAYS try to find the code before resetting — check panel door, OEM docs, and the integrator's contact info first.

faults.name: Allen-Bradley MSR Safety Relay — Configuration Fault
faults.description: Modular Safety Relay (MSR) series module is in a fault state. Configuration jumpers or DIP switches do not match the connected safety device type.
probable_causes: Configuration DIP switches set incorrectly for the application — single channel vs dual channel
Reset mode switch set wrong — auto-reset when manual-reset is required or vice versa
Safety device wired to wrong input type — light curtain wired as E-stop or vice versa
MSR module is wrong model for the application — MSR57P vs MSR127 vs MSR210 etc.
Module power supply voltage incorrect — check 24VDC rating
fix_steps: Read the MSR model number and consult the specific wiring diagram for that model
Check all DIP switches/jumpers against the application requirement — dual channel, reset type, timing
Verify wiring matches the MSR wiring diagram EXACTLY — pin-for-pin, including channel A and channel B
Check module LED indicators — most MSR modules have LEDs for power, input A, input B, and output status
Verify 24VDC supply voltage at module power terminals
If replacing an MSR, ensure replacement is the exact same model with same configuration — photo the DIP switches before removing the old unit
faults.field_trick: Before you touch an MSR safety relay, take a photo of all the DIP switches and wiring. These modules have 20 different configuration combinations and the settings are printed in microscopic text on the side of the module. One wrong DIP switch and the safety system is unreliable — photo first, change later.

faults.name: Conveyor Belt Alignment — Critical Drift
faults.description: Belt has drifted significantly off-center and is rubbing against the frame or has triggered the belt alignment switch. Risk of belt edge damage and product falling off conveyor.
probable_causes: Material buildup on one side of head or tail pulley — belt tracks toward the buildup
Pulley lagging worn unevenly — one side has more grip than the other
Idler roller seized on one side — belt pulled toward the seized roller
Belt splice not straight — angled splice causes belt to walk to one side
Conveyor frame not square or level — foundation settlement
fix_steps: Stop conveyor and inspect belt edges for fraying, cutting, or delamination
Clean head and tail pulleys — scrape off any material buildup
Check all idler rollers by hand — spin each one, feel for seized bearings
Adjust tail pulley tracking bolts — move the side the belt is tracking TOWARD by 1/4 turn
Run conveyor at slow speed and observe — make incremental 1/4 turn adjustments, wait 3 full belt rotations between adjustments
If belt continues to drift, check belt splice — a crooked splice will never track straight
faults.field_trick: The belt always tracks toward the tight side. If the belt drifts to the left, the left side of the tail pulley is tighter than the right. Loosen the left snub bolt 1/4 turn (or tighten the right 1/4 turn). Always adjust at the TAIL pulley for tracking, not the head.

faults.name: Chain Conveyor — Chain Stretch / Elongation Alarm
faults.description: Chain conveyor monitoring system detected excessive chain elongation. Chain pitch has exceeded the wear limit, increasing risk of chain skip or break.
probable_causes: Normal wear — chain elongation is cumulative and irreversible
Insufficient lubrication — pin and bushing wear accelerated by dry running
Overloading — chain loaded beyond rating causing accelerated pin wear
Sprocket teeth worn — worn sprockets accelerate chain wear
Abrasive contamination — grit or product residue grinding chain components
fix_steps: Measure chain pitch with a chain wear gauge — replace at 3% elongation for standard roller chain
Inspect drive and idler sprockets for hooked or worn tooth profiles — replace with chain
ALWAYS replace chain AND sprockets together — a new chain on worn sprockets will wear out in weeks
Check and adjust chain tensioner — take up slack, but if tensioner is at end of travel, chain is too long
Verify lubrication system is functioning — check auto-luber reservoir level and drip rate
When installing new chain, pre-stretch by running under light load for first 8 hours, then retension
faults.field_trick: Never replace just the chain or just the sprockets — always replace as a set. A new chain on worn sprockets will develop the wear pattern of the old chain within a week. The $200 you saved skipping the sprockets costs you a new chain in a month.

faults.name: Chain Conveyor — Chain Break Detected
faults.description: Chain break sensor or zero-speed switch detected that the chain has broken or the tail shaft has stopped rotating while the drive motor is running.
probable_causes: Chain link failed — fatigue crack in side plate or pin sheared
Master link (connecting link) failed — clip or cotter fell off, link separated
Excessive chain overload caused instantaneous failure
Foreign object jammed in sprocket — chain snapped from the force
Chain tensioner failed — excessive slack allowed chain to jump sprocket and snap
fix_steps: STOP the drive immediately — running a broken chain causes secondary damage to sprockets, guides, and product
Lock out / tag out the conveyor drive before entering the conveyor
Locate the break point — inspect for sheared pins, cracked plates, or separated master links
Remove damaged chain sections — pull chain out through the maintenance access
Do NOT splice a broken chain — replace the entire chain strand
Inspect sprockets for damage from the broken chain — replace any with chipped or bent teeth
Check the zero-speed sensor and wiring — verify it would have caught the failure sooner if properly calibrated
faults.field_trick: After a chain break, always find BOTH ends of the chain before restarting. A loose chain end wrapped around a shaft or sprocket can cause catastrophic damage when you restart. Walk the entire conveyor and account for every inch of the broken chain.

faults.name: Roller Conveyor — Zone Fault / Motor Roller Fault
faults.description: Motorized roller conveyor zone controller detected a fault in one or more zones. Motor roller not responding to run command, or zone sensor failure.
probable_causes: Motor roller (MDR) internal motor failed — brushless DC motor burned out
Zone controller card faulted — check LED status on zone card
Motor roller cable connector corroded or loose — 4-pin connector at roller end
Zone photo-eye blocked or failed — zone controller can't determine product presence
24VDC power supply to zone controller insufficient — voltage sag under load
fix_steps: Check zone controller LED — most show GREEN for ready, RED for fault, FLASHING for specific error codes
Verify photo-eye for the zone is operational — block and unblock, watch zone controller response
Check motor roller cable connection — disconnect and reconnect the 4-pin connector at the roller
Measure 24VDC at zone controller power input — must be 24VDC +/-10% under load
Try swapping motor roller from a known-good zone — if the fault follows the roller, roller is dead
If zone card itself is faulted, try power-cycling the zone controller
Replace motor roller if confirmed dead — note roller diameter, length, and speed rating for correct replacement
faults.field_trick: Motor rollers (MDRs) communicate with zone controllers through their cable. If you crimp a new connector on a motor roller cable and get one wire wrong, you can burn out the zone card. Always check the cable pinout against the manufacturer diagram. Itoh Denki, Interroll, and OSRI all use different color codes.

faults.name: Accumulation Conveyor — Zone Full / Back Pressure
faults.description: Accumulation conveyor has all zones occupied. Back pressure detected. Upstream conveyors should stop feeding product until space opens.
probable_causes: Downstream equipment stopped or faulted — discharge point not accepting product
Sortation diverter failed — product not being diverted, accumulating on main line
Product spacing too tight — gaps closing up causing premature zone full signals
Zone photo-eye misaligned — seeing product when zone is empty (false full)
Zone release timing incorrect — zones not releasing product in proper sequence
fix_steps: Check downstream equipment status — clear any faults or jams at the discharge end
Verify all zone photo-eyes are clean and aligned — test each zone by manually blocking and unblocking
Check product spacing at infeed — if products are touching, the singulator or gap-maker is not working
Verify zone control logic — zones should release in slug mode or singulation mode per application
If one zone is falsely showing full, check that specific photo-eye alignment and sensitivity
Check that sleep/wake function (if equipped) is working — zones should go to sleep when no product is present for energy saving
faults.field_trick: When an accumulation conveyor has all zones showing full but you can see empty zones, the problem is almost always a single dirty photo-eye. One blocked sensor tells the upstream zones 'I'm full' and the whole line backs up. Clean photo-eyes from the discharge end back toward the infeed to find the culprit.

faults.name: Sortation System — Diverter Fault
faults.description: Sortation conveyor diverter mechanism failed to actuate or did not confirm position. Product may have been sent to the wrong destination or continued on the main line.
probable_causes: Pneumatic cylinder on diverter not firing — solenoid valve stuck or air supply low
Diverter confirm sensor not seeing the diverter in position — sensor misaligned or failed
Air pressure too low for diverter speed — regulator set too low or compressor can't keep up
Diverter mechanical pivot jammed — product debris or bearing seized
PLC output to solenoid valve not firing — check output module LED
fix_steps: Check air pressure at the diverter — gauge should read 60-80 PSI minimum
Manually actuate the solenoid valve using the manual override (small button or pin on the valve body)
If cylinder moves with manual override, problem is electrical — check PLC output and solenoid valve coil
If cylinder doesn't move with manual override, problem is pneumatic — check air supply, cylinder seals, tubing
Inspect diverter confirm sensors — block/unblock and verify PLC sees the input change
Check for product debris in the diverter pivot area — clean and lubricate
Verify PLC logic is commanding the diverter at the right time based on barcode scan or tracking data
faults.field_trick: Diverter solenoid valves on high-speed sortation systems cycle millions of times a year. Replace the solenoid valve coils proactively every 18 months — they cost $15 each. A failed coil during peak shipping season means missed sorts and angry customers.

faults.name: Conveyor Merge — Collision Detected
faults.description: Merge conveyor detected a product collision at the merge point. Two products arrived at the merge zone simultaneously, causing a jam or product damage.
probable_causes: Merge timing logic error — gap between products not maintained at merge point
Upstream conveyor speed mismatch — one feed line running faster than calculated
Merge photo-eye failed — PLC doesn't know a product is already in the merge zone
Product gap creator/spacer upstream not functioning — products too close together
Slug of products released from accumulation zone simultaneously instead of singulated
fix_steps: Clear the jam at the merge point — remove damaged or misaligned products
Check all photo-eyes at the merge point — clean lenses and verify alignment
Verify both infeed conveyor speeds match the programmed values — check VFD speed parameters
Check gap-creation conveyor upstream of the merge — verify it creates minimum required gap
Review PLC merge logic timing — the hold-off timer may need adjustment for product size changes
If product size has changed, merge timing parameters may need to be updated in HMI
faults.field_trick: When merge collisions happen intermittently, put the system in manual mode and test each infeed leg one at a time. If each leg works alone but collisions happen with both running, the gap timing is too tight. Increase the gap time by 200ms and test again.

faults.name: Conveyor Belt Slip — Zero Speed Fault
faults.description: Zero-speed sensor on the conveyor tail shaft detected that the belt or chain is not moving while the drive motor is running. Indicates belt slip, chain skip, or mechanical disconnect.
probable_causes: Belt tension too low — belt slipping on drive pulley, especially when loaded
Drive pulley lagging worn smooth — no grip on belt surface
Belt or chain broken — motor spinning but belt/chain is not connected
Gearbox coupler sheared — motor and gearbox turning but output shaft is not
Zero-speed sensor failed or misaligned — not detecting shaft rotation
fix_steps: STOP the motor immediately — belt slip generates extreme heat and can start a fire on rubber belts
Check if belt/chain is intact — visual inspection along the full length
Check drive pulley engagement — look for glazing or wear on belt contact surface
Verify gearbox output shaft is turning when motor runs — listen/feel for gearbox engagement
Increase belt tension at the take-up — adjust until belt stops slipping under loaded conditions
Replace drive pulley lagging if worn smooth — herringbone or diamond pattern for best grip
Check zero-speed sensor gap — should be 2-5mm from the target (bolt head or tooth on tail shaft sprocket)
faults.field_trick: Put a piece of tape on the tail shaft and the belt. Run the conveyor and watch both. If the motor is running and the shaft turns but the belt doesn't move, the belt is slipping on the drive pulley. If the shaft isn't turning at all, something between the motor and the shaft is broken — check coupler, gearbox, chain/belt drive.

faults.name: Conveyor Take-Up — End of Travel
faults.description: Gravity or screw take-up mechanism has reached its maximum travel. Belt or chain has stretched to the point where the take-up can no longer maintain proper tension.
probable_causes: Belt/chain has stretched beyond take-up capacity — normal wear over time
Belt splice failed and belt is physically longer — splice separated under tension
Take-up mechanism jammed — corrosion, debris, or bent track preventing take-up travel
Take-up counterweight insufficient for current belt length — weight too light
New belt was not properly pre-tensioned during installation
fix_steps: Measure belt/chain elongation — determine if it needs to be shortened or replaced
For belt: cut and re-splice to remove slack — maintain proper belt tension with take-up at 50% travel
For chain: remove one or two links to shorten — reset take-up to mid-travel position
Clean and lubricate take-up slide rails — ensure free travel
Verify take-up limit switch is positioned correctly — should trip before physical end of travel
For gravity take-ups, verify counterweight is correct for belt type and width
faults.field_trick: When you re-splice a belt, always set the take-up to 40-50% travel, not fully retracted. If you set it at the minimum, the take-up has nowhere to go as the belt stretches and you'll be back cutting the belt again in 6 months. Leave room for the belt to stretch.

faults.name: Conveyor Bearing Temperature Alarm
faults.description: Bearing temperature monitoring system detected excessive temperature on a conveyor bearing. Temperature exceeded alarm threshold, indicating bearing wear or lubrication failure.
probable_causes: Bearing lubrication insufficient — grease dried out or auto-luber empty
Bearing wear — internal clearance lost, metal-to-metal contact
Bearing overloaded — conveyor loaded beyond design capacity
Misalignment between motor and gearbox or gearbox and conveyor shaft — induces axial load on bearings
Wrong grease type used — incompatible greases cause breakdown
fix_steps: Check bearing temperature with IR gun — compare to opposite side bearing for baseline
Grease the bearing with correct type and amount — do NOT over-grease, follow manufacturer schedule
Listen for bearing noise — rumbling, clicking, or high-pitched whine indicates failure modes
Check shaft alignment with straightedge or laser alignment tool — correct if needed
If bearing temp continues to rise after greasing, plan for bearing replacement at next maintenance window
When replacing bearing, inspect shaft for wear — a worn shaft will destroy the new bearing quickly
faults.field_trick: Over-greasing bearings kills more bearings than under-greasing. When you pump grease into a sealed bearing, the excess has nowhere to go — it builds pressure, pushes past the seal, and overheats the bearing. Follow the OEM schedule: usually 2-3 pumps of the grease gun, NOT a full cartridge.

faults.name: Conveyor Guard Door Interlock Open
faults.description: A guard door interlock switch on the conveyor section is open. Conveyor section is stopped due to safety interlock. Guard must be closed and reset before conveyor will restart.
probable_causes: Guard door physically open — operator accessing the conveyor for maintenance or jam clearing
Guard door closed but interlock switch not actuating — switch misaligned with actuator
Interlock switch failed — contact stuck open or spring broken
Interlock switch wiring disconnected — wire broken at terminal or in conduit
Guard door hinge sagging — door is closed but doesn't align with switch actuator anymore
fix_steps: Walk the conveyor section and visually check all guard doors — find the one that is open
If door is closed, check the interlock switch LED indicator — verify it shows actuated state
Manually press the interlock switch actuator — does the PLC input change state?
Check door alignment — close the door and verify the switch actuator key enters the switch head fully
Adjust hinge or strike plate if door has sagged and switch is not engaging
After closing guard door, press the safety reset button — most systems require deliberate reset
faults.field_trick: When operators say 'all the doors are closed' but the conveyor won't run, walk the line and try to open every guard door with the lock switch key. If one door opens without the key, the interlock switch is defeated or broken. That's your culprit.

faults.name: Conveyor Photo-Eye Alignment Fault
faults.description: Photo-eye on conveyor is misaligned or contaminated. Sensor is not reliably detecting product presence, causing false jams, missed products, or zone control issues.
probable_causes: Sensor lens contaminated — dust, product residue, condensation, or overspray
Sensor knocked out of alignment — vibration, product impact, or maintenance activity
Retro-reflector (for retro-reflective type) dirty or damaged
Sensor sensitivity set too high or too low — detecting wrong objects or missing targets
Wiring damage — cable pinched or cut, connector corroded
fix_steps: Clean sensor lens with soft cloth and mild cleaner — check both emitter and receiver (or lens and reflector)
Check sensor alignment using built-in alignment indicator (LED bar or teach function)
Verify retro-reflector is clean and not cracked — replace if damaged
Adjust sensor sensitivity using the teach button or potentiometer — test with actual product
Check sensor cable and connector for damage — replace if corroded or cut
Verify sensor mounting bracket is secure — tighten if loose, add loctite to mounting screws in high-vibration areas
faults.field_trick: Keep a can of compressed air and a microfiber cloth in your pocket when troubleshooting conveyors. 70% of conveyor sensor faults are just dirty lenses. A 30-second cleaning fixes what the PLC screen calls a 'sensor fault.' Clean first, troubleshoot second.

faults.name: Conveyor Gearbox Fault — Noise / Temperature / Oil Leak
faults.description: Conveyor drive gearbox exhibiting abnormal noise, elevated temperature, or oil leakage. Indicates internal gear wear, bearing failure, or seal degradation.
probable_causes: Gear oil level low — leak or not filled after last oil change
Gear oil degraded — water contamination, extreme age, or wrong oil type
Internal bearing failure — generates noise, heat, and metal particles in oil
Gear teeth worn or chipped — overloading or insufficient lubrication over time
Output shaft seal worn — oil leaking onto conveyor frame or floor
fix_steps: Check oil level through sight glass or dipstick — top off if low with correct oil type
Check oil condition — dark color, metallic sheen, or milky appearance indicates problems
Listen for abnormal noise — grinding means gear contact issue, whining means bearing issue
Measure gearbox surface temperature with IR gun — compare to historical readings or similar gearbox
If leaking from output seal, plan for seal replacement — requires pulling the gearbox off the conveyor
Collect an oil sample for analysis — particle count and composition tells you exactly what is wearing
Plan gearbox replacement if internal damage is confirmed — do NOT run until failure, a catastrophic gearbox failure can destroy the conveyor shaft
faults.field_trick: Put a magnet on the gearbox drain plug (magnetic drain plug). When you drain the oil, check the magnet. A few fine particles is normal. Metal flakes or chunks means gears are chipping and the gearbox has limited life left. This $5 drain plug is your early warning system.

faults.name: Conveyor E-Stop Pull Cord Activated
faults.description: Emergency stop pull cord along the conveyor section has been activated. Conveyor section is stopped. Pull cord must be reset at the activation point before conveyor can restart.
probable_causes: Operator pulled the cord intentionally — emergency situation
Product or debris snagged the pull cord — unintentional activation
Pull cord tension spring weak or broken — cord sags and triggers from vibration
Pull cord switch failed — internal mechanism triggered without cord pull
Pull cord cable broken — switch sees loss of tension as an activation (fail-safe design)
fix_steps: Walk the conveyor and find the activated pull cord section — look for pulled or slack cable
Check for any emergency condition that caused the pull — person, product jam, or fire
Reset the pull cord switch at the activation point — most have a twist-reset or push-reset button on the switch body
Verify all pull cord sections are under proper tension — cable should be taut but not over-tensioned
Replace broken or frayed pull cord cable — stainless steel cable lasts longer than galvanized in wet environments
After clearing and resetting, press the conveyor safety reset button to re-enable the section
faults.field_trick: Pull cord E-stops are supposed to activate when pulled 2-4 inches. If one keeps triggering from vibration, the tension is wrong. Walk the full length of cable and adjust turnbuckles at each support bracket until the cable is taut with about 1 inch of free movement before the switch triggers.

faults.name: Spiral Conveyor — Chain Tension / Gap Fault
faults.description: Spiral elevator or lowerator conveyor chain tension out of specification or gap between slats has exceeded maximum allowable distance.
probable_causes: Chain stretch from normal wear — spiral chain elongates over time
Take-up cylinder air pressure too low — not maintaining chain tension
Product jammed between slats — forced chain apart
Slat broken or missing — gap sensor detecting oversized gap
Drive chain or belt between motor and spiral input shaft slipping
fix_steps: Check take-up cylinder air pressure — typically 40-60 PSI depending on manufacturer
Inspect chain tension by pressing on the chain mid-span — should deflect less than 1 inch
Walk the spiral and inspect all slats — look for broken, cracked, or missing slats
Check for product jammed between slats or in the chain path
If chain is excessively stretched, remove links per manufacturer procedure — maintain minimum bend radius
Lubricate chain per manufacturer schedule — spiral chains require frequent lubrication due to continuous bending
faults.field_trick: Spiral conveyor chains wear faster on the inside of the spiral because the inner links travel a tighter radius. When measuring chain wear, always measure on the inside of a curve, not on a straight section. That's where it will fail first.

faults.name: Conveyor Barcode Scanner — No Read / Misread
faults.description: In-line barcode scanner on conveyor cannot read product barcodes. Products are being sent to a no-read reject lane or accumulating at the scan point.
probable_causes: Scanner lens dirty — dust, label adhesive, or product residue on the window
Barcode label quality poor — smudged, wrinkled, or low contrast print
Product orientation wrong — barcode not facing the scanner window
Scanner focus or distance changed — product height different than calibrated
Scanner communication lost to PLC — barcode data not reaching the sorting logic
fix_steps: Clean scanner window with approved glass cleaner and soft cloth — check daily in dusty environments
Verify barcode label quality — scan a known-good label by hand to confirm scanner is working
Check product orientation — barcode must be in the scanner's read zone and at correct angle
Verify scanner focus distance — measure gap between scanner and product surface, compare to setup spec
Check scanner to PLC communication — serial or Ethernet, verify data is being received
Run scanner diagnostics — most industrial scanners have a built-in web interface for read rate statistics
faults.field_trick: If your scanner read rate drops from 99% to 90%, don't adjust the scanner first. Check the barcode labels — label printer ribbons wear out and print quality degrades gradually. Replace the printer ribbon and the read rate will bounce back to 99%. The scanner was fine all along.

faults.name: Conveyor Motor Overtemperature — RTD/Thermistor Trip
faults.description: Conveyor motor embedded temperature sensor (RTD, PTC thermistor, or thermostat) has tripped. Motor has exceeded its safe operating temperature.
probable_causes: Motor overloaded — conveyor carrying more weight than motor is rated for
Motor cooling obstructed — TEFC fan shroud clogged, fan broken, or motor mounted in enclosed space
Ambient temperature too high — motor in hot environment near ovens, boilers, or sun exposure
Motor running at reduced speed on VFD — self-cooled motor loses cooling capacity below 30Hz
Motor bearings failing — increased friction generates heat
fix_steps: Stop the motor and let it cool — do not restart until temperature drops below the reset threshold
Check motor fan and fan shroud — clean debris, verify fan rotation
Measure motor current with clamp meter — compare to nameplate FLA
Check ambient temperature around motor — improve ventilation if in an enclosed area
If running on VFD at low speed, install a separately-powered cooling fan
Check motor bearings — feel for roughness when rotating shaft by hand
If motor trips repeatedly with normal load and good cooling, motor windings may be partially shorted — megger test
faults.field_trick: When a motor trips on overtemp and the load is normal, check the bearing closest to the fan end. That bearing runs hotter than the drive end because it's enclosed in the fan shroud. A failing fan-end bearing can heat up the motor enough to trip the thermistor without the drive-end bearing showing any symptoms.

faults.name: Conveyor Metal Detector — Contamination Reject
faults.description: In-line metal detector on conveyor detected metal contamination in a product. Product has been rejected to the reject bin. Line may be stopped pending inspection.
probable_causes: Actual metal contamination in the product — foreign material from upstream process
Metal detector sensitivity too high — detecting product effect (metallic minerals in product)
Conveyor belt splice has metal staples — detector picks up the splice on every rotation
Metal detector coils out of balance — environmental vibration or temperature change
Rejected product fell back onto the conveyor — reject mechanism not clearing product fully
fix_steps: Inspect the rejected product — open and search for metal contamination
Run test samples (ferrous, non-ferrous, stainless steel) through the detector to verify calibration
If false rejects on empty belt, check belt for metal splices or embedded metal debris
Re-balance the metal detector using the auto-learn function — run 20 good products through
Check reject mechanism — verify air pressure, solenoid, and reject confirm sensor
Verify environmental factors — vibration from nearby equipment can cause false triggers, isolate the detector on rubber mounts
faults.field_trick: If your metal detector keeps false-rejecting on the belt splice, mark the belt splice location with paint and set up a splice suppression zone in the detector controller. Most modern detectors can learn the belt splice signature and ignore it. One programming change eliminates 90% of false rejects.

faults.name: Incline/Decline Conveyor — Product Slide-Back
faults.description: Products are sliding backward on an incline conveyor when the conveyor stops. Anti-rollback or holdback device may be faulty, or belt surface grip is insufficient.
probable_causes: Backstop/holdback clutch failed — not engaging when motor stops
Belt surface worn smooth — coefficient of friction reduced from normal wear
Incline angle exceeds belt friction capability for the product type
Conveyor stops too abruptly — products have momentum that overcomes friction
Product surface changed — slippery packaging, wet product, or different material
fix_steps: Check backstop/holdback clutch — should prevent reverse rotation of drive shaft when motor is off
Test holdback by trying to turn the drive shaft backward by hand — it should lock
If belt surface is worn smooth, install rough-top belt or add cleats
Increase VFD deceleration ramp time to slow the conveyor more gradually
If holdback is failed, replace immediately — products sliding back can be a safety hazard to workers at the bottom
Consider adding a product hold-down belt or overhead belt to keep products in place on steep inclines
faults.field_trick: When replacing a holdback clutch on an incline conveyor, ALWAYS verify rotation direction before installing. A holdback installed backward will let the conveyor run in reverse at full speed when the motor stops — product avalanche. Paint a rotation arrow on the gearbox and the holdback during installation.

faults.name: AS/RS Crane X-Axis Position Error
faults.description: Storage/retrieval machine (SRM) crane horizontal travel position does not match the commanded position. Crane cannot confirm alignment with the target storage location.
probable_causes: Laser distance sensor dirty or misaligned — position feedback is inaccurate
Bar code position system misread — dirty label or damaged reflector at target location
Travel drive encoder coupling slipped — encoder reading doesn't match actual position
Crane hit a physical obstruction — limit switch tripped or safety bumper activated
Rail wheel slipping — contamination or wear on rail surface
fix_steps: Check laser position sensor alignment — clean lens with lint-free cloth
If using bar code positioning, inspect labels on the rack columns — replace any that are damaged or peeling
Verify encoder coupling on travel drive motor — tighten set screws
Jog crane to home position using manual mode — re-reference position counter
Inspect rail for contamination — oil, water, or debris causing wheel slip
Run position calibration routine — most SRM controllers have an auto-calibrate that traverses the full aisle
faults.field_trick: When an SRM crane drifts off position gradually over many cycles, it's almost always encoder wheel slippage. The rubber-tired encoder wheel on the rail wears flat over time. Replace the encoder wheel every 12 months proactively — it's a $30 part that prevents $3000 in crashed loads.

faults.name: AS/RS Crane Y-Axis (Lift) Position Error
faults.description: Crane lift mast position does not match commanded height. Cannot confirm alignment with target rack level. Load transfer cannot proceed.
probable_causes: Lift encoder or absolute position sensor fault — cable or sensor failure
Lift chain stretch causing position drift — chain elongation over time
Lift brake not releasing fully — mast creeping down after stop due to partial brake drag
Obstruction on the mast — product or packaging caught in the mast structure
Lift drive VFD fault limiting speed or torque — mast can't reach commanded position
fix_steps: Check lift position sensor — clean, verify alignment, check cable connections
Measure lift chain elongation — compare to manufacturer's wear limit
Verify lift brake operation — brake should release fully when commanded and hold when released
Inspect mast channels for obstructions — debris, bent rails, or stuck product
Check lift drive VFD for active faults or current limiting — clear and verify VFD parameters
Run lift calibration from bottom limit to top limit — recalibrate position reference points
