faults.field_trick: If the crane consistently overshoots or undershoots on the lift axis, check the lift VFD deceleration ramp. A worn lift brake combined with a fast decel ramp causes the load to bounce past the setpoint. Slow the decel ramp by 20% and retune the positioning PID — much cheaper than replacing the brake.

faults.name: AS/RS Fork Extension — Overtravel or Position Error
faults.description: Telescoping fork or shuttle mechanism extended beyond the allowed travel limit or did not reach the correct extension position for load transfer.
probable_causes: Fork extend/retract limit sensor failed — sensor not detecting fork position
Fork drive chain stretched or belt slipped — fork position offset from encoder reading
Load on fork shifted during extension — weight imbalance causing fork mechanism stress
Fork drive motor overloaded — mechanical resistance in fork rail due to contamination or wear
Fork intermediate (mid-stage) did not lock in position — latch mechanism failure
fix_steps: Check fork limit sensors — extend left, extend right, and retract home — verify each triggers at correct position
Inspect fork drive chain/belt — check tension and condition
Verify fork rails are clean and lubricated — contamination causes high friction and position errors
Check fork drive motor current during extension — compare to normal baseline
Inspect fork intermediate stage latch mechanism — verify it engages and disengages cleanly
Run fork calibration cycle — extend full left and full right in manual mode to verify travel limits
faults.field_trick: Fork telescoping mechanisms need their rails cleaned and lubricated every 3 months. If you wait until the fork stops extending, the rails are scored and need machining. A 15-minute cleaning with a rag and light oil prevents a $15,000 fork overhaul.

faults.name: AS/RS Load Presence Detection — Load Overhang
faults.description: Load overhang sensor detected that a pallet or tote is not properly positioned on the crane platform or fork. Load extends beyond the safe travel envelope.
probable_causes: Load shifted during retrieval — not centered on fork/platform
Wrong pallet size for the storage location — pallet oversize for the rack
Pallet damaged — broken stringer causing pallet to sag and overhang
Fork pickup point was off-center — crane X or Y position was slightly off during pickup
Overhang sensor misaligned — detecting overhang when load is actually centered
fix_steps: Visually inspect the load on the crane — confirm if there is actual overhang
If load is shifted, manually re-center it (follow LOTO procedure for working on crane)
Check overhang sensors — clean and verify alignment
If load overhang is genuine, do not travel — return load to origin location and investigate
Check crane positioning accuracy — a position error causes off-center pickups
Verify pallet dimensions match the AS/RS system configuration — length, width, and weight
faults.field_trick: When load overhang faults start happening on loads that used to be fine, check if the supplier changed pallet sizes. A pallet that's 1 inch wider than spec causes intermittent overhang trips. Measure 10 random pallets from the latest delivery — you'll often find the issue is the pallet, not the crane.

faults.name: AS/RS Rack Occupancy Sensor Fault
faults.description: Rack occupancy sensor at a storage location is reporting inconsistent state — showing occupied when WMS says empty, or empty when WMS says occupied.
probable_causes: Sensor dirty or misaligned — rack environment is dusty or sensors were bumped during maintenance
Sensor failed — retro-reflective sensor or through-beam emitter/receiver degraded
Inventory data in WMS/WCS is incorrect — manual move not recorded in system
Pallet positioned incorrectly in rack — load too far back or forward to trigger sensor
Sensor cable damaged — cable routed through rack structure where it can be hit by forks
fix_steps: Physically verify the storage location — confirm if a load is actually present
Clean the occupancy sensor and reflector — rack sensors accumulate dust and debris over time
Check sensor alignment — emitter should be aimed directly at reflector on opposite side of rack bay
If inventory mismatch, update WMS/WCS to match physical reality
Inspect sensor cable routing — re-route if in a location where forklift or crane forks can damage it
Replace sensor if cleaning and alignment don't resolve — note part number and beam type for reorder
faults.field_trick: Rack occupancy sensors fail in predictable patterns — the bottom two rack levels fail 5x more often because forklift exhaust and dust settle at ground level. Clean bottom-level sensors monthly, upper-level sensors quarterly. This simple schedule eliminates 80% of occupancy sensor faults.

faults.name: AS/RS Traffic Control — Aisle Interlock Violation
faults.description: AS/RS traffic management detected an aisle interlock violation. Two cranes attempting to enter the same aisle, or a crane in an aisle where personnel access is active.
probable_causes: Aisle interlock sensor failed — not detecting crane presence in aisle
WCS traffic manager software lost communication with one or both cranes
Personnel access gate opened while crane is in the aisle — safety interlock activated
Crane overran the aisle boundary — positioning system did not stop crane at aisle limit
Manual mode operation bypassed traffic management interlocks
fix_steps: Verify both cranes' positions — confirm no actual collision risk before proceeding
Check aisle boundary sensors — each aisle should have presence detection at entry/exit
Verify WCS communication to all cranes — check network connections
If personnel access gate triggered, walk the aisle to verify no personnel present before clearing
Do NOT override traffic interlocks without physically verifying the aisle is clear
Reset traffic violation from WCS operator station after confirming safe conditions
faults.field_trick: When a traffic interlock trips, NEVER clear it from the control room without physically verifying the aisle. AS/RS aisles are tight — if a person is in the aisle and you clear the interlock, the crane will move. Take the 5-minute walk to visually confirm the aisle is clear. Every time. No exceptions.

faults.name: AS/RS Sequence Error — Incomplete Command
faults.description: Crane received a storage or retrieval command but cannot complete the sequence. One or more steps in the command sequence failed or timed out.
probable_causes: Previous command did not complete — crane still has a load from a failed prior cycle
Destination location is occupied — WMS shows empty but sensor shows full
Source location is empty — WMS shows occupied but crane found no load
Fork cycle timeout — fork extension or retraction took longer than allowed
Communication lost between WCS and crane controller mid-sequence
fix_steps: Check crane current status — determine which step in the sequence failed
If crane has an unexpected load, identify the load and determine correct destination
Verify source and destination locations physically — match to WMS data
If fork timeout, check fork mechanism for mechanical issues
Cancel the incomplete command in WCS and re-issue after resolving the root cause
Resynchronize WMS inventory data with physical reality using a spot-check cycle count
faults.field_trick: When sequence errors happen frequently, the root cause is almost always inventory accuracy. If your WMS says a location is empty and the crane arrives to find a load there, the previous putaway wasn't confirmed. Focus on closing the loop on every transaction — every put, every pick, confirmed with sensor feedback.

faults.name: AS/RS Crane Rail Alignment — Wheel Wear
faults.description: Crane exhibiting abnormal vibration, noise, or position drift indicating rail or wheel wear issues. May trigger position faults or cause premature component failure.
probable_causes: Guide wheel wear — polyurethane guide wheels worn flat or cracked
Rail joint misalignment — expansion joint opened or rail sections shifted
Rail surface contamination — oil, water, or debris on travel surface
Wheel bearing failure — rumbling noise from travel wheels
Top guide rail loosened from rack structure — mounting bolts vibrated loose
fix_steps: Visually inspect all crane wheels — travel wheels and guide wheels — look for flat spots, cracks, or missing material
Check rail joints — any gap or step at a joint causes impact loading on wheels and structure
Clean rail surface — remove oil, debris, and any product that fell from racks
Measure wheel diameter and compare to new spec — replace wheels at 90% of original diameter
Check top guide rail mounting — tighten all bolts and add loctite in high-vibration areas
If crane vibration is severe, stop operation until root cause is resolved — vibration damages encoders, wiring, and structural connections
faults.field_trick: Listen to the crane during travel — a healthy crane makes a steady hum. A clicking or thumping at regular intervals means a wheel has a flat spot. A grinding sound means a bearing is failing. Walk alongside the crane at slow speed and you can pinpoint which wheel by ear alone.

faults.name: AS/RS Infeed/Outfeed Conveyor — Handoff Fault
faults.description: Load transfer between the AS/RS infeed/outfeed conveyor and the crane platform failed. Load did not complete the handoff within the allowed time.
probable_causes: Conveyor did not position load correctly at the handoff point — load short or overshot
Crane platform position does not align with conveyor — crane X or Y position off
Load is jammed or stuck on the conveyor infeed — damaged pallet, broken conveyor roller
Handoff sensor sequence not completed — load present sensors did not transition in correct order
Conveyor motor tripped during handoff — VFD fault or motor overload
fix_steps: Check load position on conveyor — is it at the correct handoff point?
Verify crane platform is aligned with the conveyor height and position
Inspect handoff zone for physical obstructions — debris, pallet fragments, wrapping material
Check all handoff sensors — load present on conveyor, load clear on crane, alignment sensors
Verify conveyor motor is running — check VFD status for faults
If load is partially transferred, manually complete or retract the load (follow LOTO procedures)
faults.field_trick: The handoff point between conveyor and crane is the highest-maintenance area in any AS/RS system. Put it on a weekly inspection schedule — check roller alignment, sensor cleanliness, and conveyor chain tension at the handoff zone. 40% of AS/RS downtime happens at this one point.

faults.name: AS/RS WCS Communication Loss — Crane Offline
faults.description: Warehouse Control System (WCS) lost communication with the crane controller. Crane is operating in local mode or is stopped, not accepting commands from the WCS.
probable_causes: Ethernet network connection between WCS server and crane PLC lost — cable, switch, or NIC failure
Crane PLC faulted or rebooted — communication dropped during controller restart
WCS server application crashed or hung — not sending/receiving messages
Network bandwidth saturated — excessive traffic on shared network infrastructure
Firewall or VLAN change on the IT network blocking WCS-to-crane traffic
fix_steps: Check WCS application status — verify the WCS software is running and responsive
Ping the crane PLC from the WCS server — verify basic network connectivity
Check crane PLC status — controller should be in RUN mode with no major faults
Check network switch ports — verify link LEDs on both ends of the connection
If IT recently made network changes, check VLAN assignments and firewall rules
Restart WCS communication service if application is running but connection is stale — this often reconnects without a full WCS restart
faults.field_trick: In most AS/RS installations, the crane PLC network and the office IT network share infrastructure (same switches, same cable plant). When IT pushes a switch firmware update or changes a VLAN at 2 AM, it can take the crane offline. Always get on the IT change notification list so you know about network changes BEFORE your crane stops talking.

faults.name: AS/RS Crane Travel/Lift Brake Fault
faults.description: Crane travel or lift motor brake is not releasing or not engaging properly. Brake feedback switch indicates incorrect state.
probable_causes: Brake coil burned out — cannot energize to release brake
Brake air gap too large — brake not fully releasing, causing drag and overheating
Brake feedback switch failed or misadjusted — reporting wrong brake state
Brake disc worn — insufficient friction material for full holding torque
24VDC or 208VAC supply to brake coil missing — blown fuse or relay failure in brake circuit
fix_steps: Check brake coil voltage — measure at the coil terminals when commanded to release
Manually release the brake (using manual release lever) — if brake mechanism moves freely, coil is the issue
Check brake air gap — most brakes have an adjustment nut for air gap setting (typically 0.5-1.0mm)
Inspect brake disc for wear — measure friction material thickness against minimum spec
Check brake feedback switch — verify it changes state when brake is released
If brake coil is burned, check what caused the over-current — usually a stuck brake disc or seized mechanism
faults.field_trick: AS/RS crane brakes have a manual release lever for emergencies — usually a hex bolt or lever on the brake housing. If a crane is stuck at height with a failed brake, you can manually release the brake to lower the mast. But someone must be ready to catch the load with a forklift — the mast will drop under gravity when the brake releases.

faults.name: AS/RS Fire Detection / Suppression Activation
faults.description: Fire detection system in the AS/RS rack structure has activated. Crane operations are halted. Fire suppression may have been deployed.
probable_causes: Actual fire — stored product or packaging ignited
Heat detector or smoke detector false alarm — dust, steam, or temperature spike
Sprinkler head accidentally activated — mechanical impact or corrosion
Fire detection system sensor fault — detector end-of-life or contamination
Fire alarm pull station accidentally activated near the AS/RS area
fix_steps: FIRST: Confirm if there is an actual fire — do NOT assume it is a false alarm
If fire confirmed, follow facility emergency action plan — evacuate, call fire department
If confirmed false alarm, identify which detector zone activated — check fire alarm panel annunciator
Check the triggered detector — replace if contaminated, end-of-life, or physically damaged
If sprinkler head activated, water damage to stored product is likely — begin assessment immediately
Fire system reset must be performed by qualified fire alarm technician
Crane restart requires fire system all-clear AND manual verification of AS/RS structure integrity
faults.field_trick: After a sprinkler activation in an AS/RS, check the rack structure for water damage to cardboard loads BEFORE running the crane. Water-soaked pallets of cardboard weigh 3x their normal weight and can exceed rack capacity. A collapsed rack section from overweight wet pallets is a catastrophic secondary event.

faults.name: AS/RS Shuttle Vehicle — Battery / Charging Fault
faults.description: Shuttle vehicle in a shuttle-based AS/RS system reports a battery fault, low charge state, or charging contact failure. Shuttle cannot operate until power is restored.
probable_causes: Battery state of charge critically low — shuttle ran too many cycles without returning to charge position
Charging rail contacts dirty or corroded — shuttle not receiving charge current
Battery management system (BMS) fault — cell imbalance or temperature fault
Charger power supply failed — no charge current available at the charging rail
Battery end of life — lithium or lead-acid cells degraded below useful capacity
fix_steps: Check shuttle battery voltage from the vehicle controller display — compare to minimum operating voltage
Navigate shuttle to a charging position — if it has enough charge to move
Clean charging contacts on the shuttle and on the rail — use fine sandpaper or contact cleaner
Measure charge voltage at the rail — verify charger is outputting correct voltage
Check BMS status — look for cell balance warnings or temperature flags
If battery will not accept charge, plan for battery replacement — match exact chemistry and capacity spec
faults.field_trick: Shuttle battery charging contacts wear out faster than you'd expect because the shuttle vibrates when running. The contacts arc slightly on every micro-disconnect. Replace charging contacts proactively every 12 months and clean them every month. Corroded contacts cause incomplete charging, which shortens battery life.

faults.name: AS/RS Overweight Load Detected
faults.description: Load weight measurement at the AS/RS infeed station or on the crane platform exceeded the maximum allowable weight. System will not store an overweight load.
probable_causes: Load genuinely overweight — product or pallet exceeds AS/RS rated capacity
Scale calibration drift — load cells reading high due to calibration error
Water or moisture added weight — product absorbed moisture during transit or storage
Wrong product loaded on pallet — heavier product than system expects
Scale platform fouled — debris under the platform adding false weight
fix_steps: Verify load weight with an independent scale (forklift scale or floor scale) — confirm actual weight
If genuinely overweight, reject the load — do NOT override the weight limit
If scale reads high, calibrate using a known test weight — most AS/RS scales have a calibration procedure
Check scale platform for debris underneath — clean the area under and around the load cells
Verify scale load cells are not damaged — inspect for physical impact damage
Update WMS with actual weights for the product SKU to prevent future overweight loads
faults.field_trick: AS/RS cranes are rated for a specific load weight, and that rating includes the pallet. If your system is rated for 2,500 lbs and your product weighs 2,400 lbs, you only have 100 lbs left for the pallet. Hardwood pallets weigh 50-75 lbs, but a wet pallet can weigh 120+ lbs. Weigh your pallets, not just your product.

faults.name: Case Erector — Case Magazine Empty / Low
faults.description: Case erector magazine is empty or at the low level sensor. Machine will stop when the last case blank is consumed. Operator must reload case blanks.
probable_causes: Magazine not refilled in time — operator missed the low-level warning
Low-level sensor failed — did not alert operator before empty
Wrong case size loaded — blanks are jammed in the magazine
Magazine guide rails not adjusted for case blank size — blanks are skewing or falling over
Case blank quality poor — warped or moisture-damaged blanks won't feed properly
fix_steps: Load fresh case blanks into the magazine — verify correct case size and orientation
Check magazine guide rail adjustment — rails should be snug to blank width with 1/8 inch clearance
Verify low-level sensor is functioning — block and unblock to confirm signal to PLC
If blanks are jamming, inspect for warped or moisture-damaged blanks — remove and discard defective blanks
Adjust vacuum suction cups if blanks not separating properly — cups must grip cleanly without double-feeding
Restart the erector cycle after loading — most require a manual start after empty magazine
faults.field_trick: Case blanks from the bottom of the pallet are almost always moisture-damaged and warped. They jam the magazine every time. Train operators to inspect the bottom 2 layers of blanks and discard any that are soft or warped. This prevents 90% of case erector jams.

faults.name: Case Erector — Vacuum Pickup Failure
faults.description: Case erector vacuum pickup system cannot reliably grab and erect case blanks. Blanks are being dropped, double-fed, or not opening to correct form.
probable_causes: Vacuum cups worn or cracked — cannot form seal on case blank surface
Vacuum pump/generator performance degraded — insufficient vacuum level
Case blank surface coated or glossy — vacuum cups can't seal on slick material
Vacuum tubing cracked, kinked, or disconnected — air leak in the vacuum circuit
Case blank quality issue — inconsistent surface, die cut tabs interfering with cup seal
fix_steps: Check vacuum level at the pickup cups — should pull at least 18 inches Hg for most applications
Inspect vacuum cups — replace any that are cracked, permanently deformed, or hard/brittle
Check all vacuum tubing from generator to cups — replace any that are kinked, cracked, or collapsed
Test vacuum generator — most Venturi generators require 60-80 PSI shop air supply
Try a different vacuum cup material — flat cups for smooth surfaces, bellows cups for textured or warped surfaces
Verify case blank specifications match the erector setup — correct size, flute direction, and surface treatment
faults.field_trick: Keep a pack of spare vacuum cups in the machine's tool cabinet. Cups are consumable items that should be changed every 2-3 months on high-speed erectors. When a cup starts to crack, it doesn't fail completely — it just slowly loses vacuum until the erector becomes unreliable. Replace all cups at once on a schedule.

faults.name: Case Sealer — Tape Application Fault
faults.description: Case sealer tape head is not applying tape correctly. Tape is missing, short, wrinkled, not adhering, or cutting improperly.
probable_causes: Tape roll empty or depleted — needs replacement
Tape tension incorrect — too tight causes tape to curl, too loose causes wrinkles
Tape cut blade dull — not cutting cleanly, leaving tails or pulling tape off case
Tape head pressure rollers worn — not pressing tape firmly onto case surface
Case height adjustments wrong — tape head not making proper contact with case flaps
fix_steps: Load a new roll of tape — verify correct width (typically 2 or 3 inch) and adhesive type
Adjust tape tension spring or knob per manufacturer spec — tape should pull smoothly without jerking
Replace tape cut blade — most use a serrated or straight blade that is a consumable part
Inspect and replace pressure rollers if worn or glazed — they should be compliant/soft rubber
Adjust case height guides to match the current case size — top and bottom tape heads must contact flaps
Verify case flaps are folding flat — if flaps spring back up, the folding plow or compression section needs adjustment
faults.field_trick: When tape wrinkles on the case, everyone adjusts the tape head. But 80% of the time the problem is the case — the flaps aren't folding flat because the compression section is set too wide. Tighten the compression rails first so the flaps are fully closed and flat BEFORE the tape head. Then the tape goes on smooth.

faults.name: Stretch Wrapper — Film Break During Cycle
faults.description: Stretch wrapper film broke during the wrapping cycle. Load is partially wrapped. Film tail must be re-attached and cycle restarted or completed manually.
probable_causes: Film pre-stretch ratio too high — film stretched beyond its elongation capacity
Sharp corner on the pallet load — film punctured on the corner
Film quality poor — inconsistent thickness, cold film in unheated warehouse
Film carriage speed too fast relative to turntable rotation — excessive pull force on film
Film tail clamp not holding — film pulls free from the clamp and breaks at the start of the wrap
fix_steps: Manually wrap the film tail around the load to secure the partially wrapped portion
Reduce pre-stretch ratio by 10-20% and test — typical good stretch film handles 200-250% pre-stretch
Check for sharp load corners — add corner boards to protect the film at corners
Slow down the film carriage speed or increase turntable rotation to reduce film tension
Inspect film pre-stretch rollers — if rollers are worn or contaminated, film slips and stretches unevenly
If film is cold (below 50F/10C), let the film warm to room temperature before using — cold film is brittle
faults.field_trick: When film breaks consistently at the same point in the wrap cycle (usually the bottom corner of the load), the problem is the load, not the wrapper. Add a cardboard corner board on the sharp corner where the film breaks. A $0.10 corner board prevents film waste and downtime.

faults.name: Stretch Wrapper — Turntable Motor Fault
faults.description: Stretch wrapper turntable motor has faulted. Turntable will not rotate. VFD driving the turntable motor shows a fault code.
probable_causes: Turntable VFD tripped on overcurrent — overweight load or mechanical binding
Turntable bearing failure — turntable not spinning freely
Turntable drive chain or belt broken — motor runs but turntable doesn't spin
Pallet or product jammed against the wrapper frame — preventing turntable rotation
VFD parameter corruption — turntable VFD lost its programming
fix_steps: Check turntable VFD display for fault code — clear and attempt restart
Inspect turntable area for physical obstructions — remove any product, pallet debris, or foreign objects
With no load on the turntable, verify it rotates freely by pushing it by hand
Check turntable drive chain/belt — inspect for break, excessive slack, or damage
If turntable is hard to rotate by hand, inspect the turntable bearing — plan for replacement if seized
Verify VFD parameters are correct — compare to commissioning sheet or backup parameter file
faults.field_trick: Stretch wrapper turntable bearings live in the worst possible environment — they sit on the floor, get hit by forklifts, and never get lubricated. Every 6 months, jack up the turntable and spin it by hand. If it feels gritty or doesn't coast, grease it or replace it. A $300 bearing replacement is cheaper than $3000 in overtime from wrapper downtime during peak shipping.

faults.name: Label Applicator — Missing Label / No Label Detected
faults.description: Print-and-apply label applicator failed to apply a label to the product or case. Missing label sensor did not detect a label on the product after application.
probable_causes: Label roll empty — no more labels on the roll
Label peeled from liner but did not transfer to product — vacuum pad failed to hold label
Label applicator air cylinder did not fire — compressed air supply low or solenoid fault
Label printer ran out of ribbon — thermal transfer ribbon depleted
Product was not in position when label was applied — timing/sensor issue
fix_steps: Check label roll — load a new roll if empty, thread through the printer and peel bar
Check thermal transfer ribbon supply — load new ribbon if depleted
Verify compressed air supply to the applicator — should be 60-80 PSI at the regulator
Check vacuum pad and tamp cylinder operation — manually trigger a label apply cycle
Clean the missing label verification sensor — typically a retro-reflective photo-eye after the apply point
Verify product triggering sensor — product must be in the correct position before the apply signal fires
faults.field_trick: When a label applicator starts missing every 5th or 10th label, the problem is usually the label peeling angle at the peel bar. The peel bar angle must be sharp enough (150-170 degrees) that the label separates from the liner cleanly. If the bar has been bumped or shifted, re-adjust it by 5 degrees and the miss rate drops to zero.

faults.name: Checkweigher — Weight Reject / Out of Tolerance
faults.description: In-line checkweigher detected a product outside the acceptable weight range. Product has been rejected. May indicate fill level issue, missing components, or checkweigher calibration drift.
probable_causes: Product genuinely underweight or overweight — fill station issue, missing component, or double-feed
Checkweigher calibration drifted — load cell zero offset or span error
Conveyor vibration affecting checkweigher accuracy — external vibration source nearby
Product not stable on the weigh belt — bouncing or sliding during weighment
Checkweigher speed too fast for the product weight and settling time
fix_steps: Verify rejected product weight with a bench scale — confirm if the reject was valid
Run a checkweigher calibration check with a known test weight — note zero and span accuracy
If calibration is off, perform full calibration procedure using certified test weights
Check for vibration sources — compressors, conveyors, or foot traffic near the checkweigher
Verify product is stable on the weigh belt — check belt speed and product spacing
If vibration is unavoidable, adjust the digital filter settings — increase filter strength (reduces speed but improves accuracy)
faults.field_trick: Checkweighers hate vibration. If yours suddenly starts rejecting good products, check if someone put a new machine, compressor, or forklift traffic route near the checkweigher. Isolating a checkweigher from the floor with vibration damping pads improves accuracy by 50% in a typical plant environment.

faults.name: Metal Detector — Sensitivity Fault / Auto-Balance Fail
faults.description: In-line metal detector cannot achieve balance or sensitivity is outside the validated range. Detector cannot reliably detect contaminants at the required level.
probable_causes: Environmental interference — new motor, VFD, or electrical equipment installed nearby
Detector aperture contaminated — metal debris stuck in the throat of the detector
Detector coils degraded — moisture or physical damage to the search head
Product effect too high — metallic minerals in the product are swamping the detector signal
Detector electronics fault — amplifier or signal processing board failure
fix_steps: Run detector auto-balance cycle — let it recalibrate to current environmental conditions
Clean inside the detector aperture thoroughly — remove any embedded metal fragments or debris
Run test standards (ferrous, non-ferrous, stainless steel) through the detector — verify detection at required sensitivity
Check for new electrical equipment installed within 6 feet of the detector — move if possible, or add shielding
If product effect is too high, reduce detector sensitivity or switch to multi-frequency detection mode if available
Contact detector manufacturer service if auto-balance consistently fails — may indicate internal component failure
faults.field_trick: VFDs installed near metal detectors are the number one cause of 'random' false rejects and balance faults. The VFD output generates EMI that the metal detector picks up as a signal. If you can't move the VFD, install an output line reactor on the VFD and add a shielded cable between the VFD and motor. Distance helps too — every foot of separation reduces interference.

faults.name: Heat Seal / Impulse Seal Fault — Weak or Incomplete Seal
faults.description: Heat sealing or impulse sealing system producing weak, incomplete, or inconsistent seals. Product may leak or seal may fail during handling.
probable_causes: Seal bar temperature too low — heater element partially burned out or temperature controller failing
Seal bar surface contaminated — product residue on the seal bar preventing clean seal
Seal dwell time too short — jaw closing time insufficient for the film thickness
Film type changed — new film requires different temperature or pressure settings
Seal bar jaw pressure uneven — one side makes contact but the other doesn't
fix_steps: Check seal bar temperature with a contact thermometer — compare to the setpoint on the controller
Clean seal bars and Teflon tape with a Teflon-safe cleaner — replace Teflon tape if burned or torn
Increase seal dwell time by 0.1-0.2 seconds and test seal integrity
Verify film specifications — check temperature range required for the current film type
Check jaw pressure alignment — shim or adjust jaw parallel to ensure even pressure across the full seal width
If heater element resistance has increased (partial burnout), replace the element — measure ohms and compare to spec
faults.field_trick: When seals are good on one end but bad on the other, the seal bar is not parallel. Stick a piece of carbon paper between the jaws and close them. The carbon impression shows you exactly where the jaws make contact and where they don't. Adjust until the impression is even across the full width.

faults.name: Filling Machine — Fill Level Out of Tolerance
faults.description: Filling machine is not achieving target fill weight or volume consistently. Products are overfilled or underfilled beyond the acceptable tolerance band.
probable_causes: Fill nozzle clogged or partially blocked — restricting flow on some fill heads
Product supply pressure or level fluctuating — inconsistent head pressure to the filler
Fill valve timing drift — solenoid valve response time changed due to wear
Product temperature changed — viscosity change affects flow rate at the same valve timing
Load cell or flow meter drift — fill measurement sensor needs recalibration
fix_steps: Spot-check fill weights on a bench scale — check 10 consecutive fills to assess variation
Clean all fill nozzles — remove and inspect for product buildup or obstructions
Check product supply pressure at the filler manifold — should be consistent within 2%
If volumetric filling, check product temperature — recalculate volume-to-weight conversion if temp changed
Calibrate the fill measurement system (load cells, flow meters) using certified test weights or volumes
If using time-based filling, check solenoid valve response — replace valve if slow to open or close
faults.field_trick: Fillers that were accurate yesterday and are off today — check the product temperature first. A 10-degree temperature change on a viscous product changes the flow rate by 15-20%. Honey at 70F fills completely different than honey at 80F. If the product temp varies, you need temperature-compensated filling.

faults.name: Shrink Tunnel — Temperature Fault / Uneven Shrink
faults.description: Shrink tunnel producing uneven, incomplete, or excessive shrink on products. Film is not conforming uniformly to the product contour.
probable_causes: Tunnel temperature set incorrectly for the film type — PVC, polyolefin, and PE all have different shrink temperatures
Heater element burned out — one zone not heating, causing cold spot in the tunnel
Air circulation fan failed — hot air not circulating evenly around the product
Conveyor speed through tunnel too fast — product not in the heat zone long enough
Film wrapping not uniform — too much or too little film on one side before entering tunnel
fix_steps: Check tunnel temperature at multiple points — use a thermocouple probe at the inlet, middle, and outlet
Verify heater elements are all functioning — measure resistance on each element
Check circulation fans — all should be running at full speed during operation
Adjust conveyor speed through the tunnel — slower speed = more heat exposure = tighter shrink
Verify film type matches the tunnel temperature setting — PVC: 275-325F, polyolefin: 300-375F
Check for air leaks at tunnel inlet and outlet — curtains or air curtains should minimize heat escape
faults.field_trick: If one side of the product shrinks tight and the other side is loose, the problem is the upstream wrapper, not the tunnel. The loose side has too much film, so even though it shrinks, there's excess material that bunches up. Fix the wrapper so film distribution is even, then the tunnel produces uniform shrink.

faults.name: Cartoner — Product Insert Fault / Empty Carton
faults.description: Cartoning machine detected an empty carton (no product insert) or product not fully inserted into the carton. Machine stopped to prevent shipping empty packages.
probable_causes: Product feed not synchronized with carton — product arrived late or not at all
Product pusher mechanism failed — cylinder not extending to push product into carton
Product jammed in the feed chute — stuck or oriented incorrectly
Carton was not fully opened — partially collapsed carton prevented product entry
Verification sensor failed — not correctly detecting product inside carton
fix_steps: Clear the jammed or empty carton from the machine
Check product feed — verify products are arriving at the insertion point consistently
Check pusher cylinder operation — manually trigger and observe full extension
Inspect product feed chute for obstructions or misoriented products
Verify carton opening mechanism — vacuum cups, guide rails, and plows must open carton fully
Check insert verification sensor — typically a photo-eye or proximity sensor inside the carton station
faults.field_trick: When a cartoner starts producing empty cartons intermittently, watch the product infeed belt-to-bucket timing. Product is probably arriving at the bucket 50ms late, so the bucket is in position but the product hasn't dropped in yet. Adjust the infeed belt speed or the product release timing by small increments.

faults.name: Banding / Strapping Machine — Strap Feed Fault
faults.description: Automatic strapping machine cannot feed, tension, or seal the strap. Strap may be jammed in the feed track, not tensioning properly, or weld/seal failing.
probable_causes: Strap coil empty — needs replacement
Strap jammed in the feed track or strap chute — strap kinked or curled and stuck
Strap tensioning motor or clutch worn — cannot pull adequate tension
Weld/seal blade worn or contaminated — heat seal blade not joining strap ends
Strap width changed — wrong strap loaded for the machine strap track size
fix_steps: Check strap supply coil — load new coil if empty, thread through the strap track
Clear any strap jams from the strap track — open the track guides and pull out kinked strap
Check strap tension setting — adjust tensioning force to match the load requirements
Inspect the weld/seal blade — replace if worn, pitted, or contaminated with melted strap residue
Verify strap size matches the machine spec — width and thickness must match the strap track
Lubricate the strap track per manufacturer schedule — dry tracks cause strap friction and jams
faults.field_trick: Strap jams in the strap track are caused by kinked strap 99% of the time. When you load a new coil, unwind the first 3 layers and discard them — the outer layers get kinked from storage and handling. Start with clean, un-kinked strap and jams drop to near zero.

faults.name: Case Printer / Date Coder — Print Quality Fault
faults.description: Inkjet or thermal date coder on the packaging line producing illegible, faded, or missing print. Product traceability and date coding not meeting quality standards.
probable_causes: Ink supply low or empty — needs refill or cartridge replacement
Print head nozzle clogged — dried ink or contamination blocking one or more nozzles
Print head distance from product changed — product height variation or print head shifted
Ink type wrong for the substrate — ink not adhering to the packaging material
Encoder signal lost — inkjet not receiving product speed signal, distorting print
fix_steps: Check ink level — refill or replace cartridge
Run print head purge/cleaning cycle from the printer menu — clears clogged nozzles
Check print head distance to product surface — typical gap is 5-15mm for inkjet
Verify ink type is correct for the substrate (porous vs non-porous, hot surface vs cold surface)
Check product speed encoder — verify encoder is spinning when conveyor moves, and signal reaches the printer
If print quality doesn't improve after purge, manually clean the print head face plate with manufacturer-approved solvent
faults.field_trick: When an inkjet printer nozzle clogs, don't run 10 purge cycles in a row — you'll waste a ton of ink. Run one purge, print a test pattern, evaluate. If still clogged, soak the print head in manufacturer cleaning solution for 10 minutes, then purge once. The soak dissolves dried ink that purging alone can't clear.

faults.name: Palletizer Layer Forming — Row Push Fault
faults.description: Palletizer layer forming area failed to push a complete row of products. Row pusher did not complete its stroke, or products were not properly positioned before the push.
probable_causes: Product jammed in the layer forming table — misaligned or fallen product blocking the row
Row pusher cylinder not extending fully — air pressure low or cylinder seal leaking
Row counter photo-eye missed a product — incorrect row count caused premature push
Product orientation wrong — turned product doesn't fit in the row pattern
Row guide rails too tight or too loose for product size — products jamming or falling out of alignment
fix_steps: Clear the jammed products from the layer forming table
Check row pusher cylinder — manually fire it from the PLC or valve override to verify full stroke
Verify air pressure at the cylinder regulator — typically 60-80 PSI
Check row counter photo-eye — clean lens and verify alignment with product detection zone
Adjust layer forming table guide rails to match product dimensions — snug with 1/4 inch clearance
If product orientation is inconsistent, check upstream turner/orienter mechanism
faults.field_trick: When a palletizer has intermittent row push problems, watch the product flow onto the layer forming table. If products arrive with inconsistent gaps, the row fills unevenly and the push misaligns items. Fix the product spacing at the infeed conveyor, not at the palletizer. The palletizer can only work with what you give it.

faults.name: Palletizer Layer Forming — Pattern Error
faults.description: Palletizer detected that the formed layer does not match the programmed pattern. Product count, orientation, or spacing is incorrect for the current recipe.
probable_causes: Wrong recipe selected on HMI — pattern doesn't match the current product
Product size changed — new product is different dimensions than programmed pattern
Layer forming photo-eye miscounting — missed product or double-counted
Row turning mechanism not rotating products to correct orientation for alternating layers
Product damaged or deformed — doesn't hold shape in the pattern
fix_steps: Verify the correct recipe/pattern is selected on the HMI for the current product
Check product dimensions against the programmed pattern — width, length, and height must match
Inspect layer forming photo-eyes — clean and verify accurate product counting
Check product turner/rotator — verify it rotates products correctly for alternating layers
If product dimensions changed, update the palletizer recipe with new product measurements
Run a test layer in slow mode — watch each product position to identify where the pattern breaks down
faults.field_trick: When switching products on a palletizer, ALWAYS run the first 3 layers in slow mode and watch. Programmers often set up patterns in the office and don't account for real-world product variation. A case that's 1/4 inch wider than the drawing can cause every layer to be off by an inch at the far edge.

faults.name: Palletizer Slip Sheet Dispenser — Feed Fault
faults.description: Slip sheet (tier sheet) dispenser cannot pick or place a slip sheet between pallet layers. Slip sheets are not dispensing or are double-feeding.
probable_causes: Slip sheet magazine empty — needs reloading
Vacuum cups on the slip sheet picker worn or leaking — cannot grip sheet
Slip sheets sticking together — static, humidity, or sheet quality issue
Slip sheet picker alignment off — not centered over the magazine
Compressed air supply low — vacuum generator not producing adequate vacuum
fix_steps: Reload slip sheet magazine — verify correct sheet size and material
Inspect vacuum cups — replace if worn, cracked, or deformed
If sheets are sticking together, fan the stack before loading — or install a sheet separator/air knife
Check vacuum level at the pickup cups — minimum 18 inches Hg for cardboard sheets
Verify picker arm alignment — center over the magazine, correct height for pickup
Check air pressure to the vacuum generator — minimum 60 PSI typically required
faults.field_trick: Slip sheets stick together in humid environments. Install a small fan blowing across the top of the slip sheet magazine to keep the top sheets dry and separated. A $20 fan prevents most double-feed issues in humid climates or seasonal humidity changes.

faults.name: Palletizer Pallet Dispenser — Empty Pallet Feed Fault
faults.description: Empty pallet dispenser cannot reliably separate and feed single pallets to the palletizing station. Pallets are double-feeding, not separating, or not advancing.
probable_causes: Pallet stack empty — forklift needs to load new stack of empty pallets
Pallet separator forks not engaging — hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders not firing
Pallet quality inconsistent — different sizes, broken stringers, or non-standard pallets mixed in the stack
Pallet dispenser photo-eyes blocked — not detecting pallet presence correctly
Dispenser conveyor or chain drive not advancing pallet to the palletizing station
fix_steps: Check pallet supply — load a new stack of empty pallets
Verify all pallets in the stack are the same size and type — remove any oddballs
Check separator fork operation — manually trigger from PLC to verify cylinder extends and retracts
Inspect pallet dispenser photo-eyes — clean lenses and verify correct detection of single pallet
Check dispenser conveyor chain — verify it advances the pallet to the correct position
If double-feeding, inspect the pallet separator gap — adjust for the actual pallet deck thickness
faults.field_trick: Pallet dispensers fail because of bad pallets. A stack of pallets with one broken stringer halfway down will jam the dispenser every time. Before loading a stack, kick the side of every pallet in the stack. If one shifts or flexes, pull it out. 5 seconds of inspection prevents 30 minutes of downtime.

faults.name: Palletizer Gripper / Vacuum Head — Pickup Failure
faults.description: Palletizer layer pickup gripper or vacuum head failed to grip and lift a complete layer. Layer dropped, shifted, or was not picked at all.
probable_causes: Vacuum level insufficient — vacuum pump worn, filter clogged, or leak in the manifold
Vacuum cups worn or damaged — not forming seal on product surface
Product surface too porous or textured for vacuum pickup — air leaks through product surface
Gripper clamp fingers not closing fully — cylinder pressure low or mechanical obstruction
Layer not formed correctly — gaps between products prevent full vacuum seal
fix_steps: Check vacuum level at the gripper head — should be at least 18 inches Hg for most applications
Inspect all vacuum cups — replace any that are cracked, torn, or permanently deformed
Check vacuum pump oil level and filter — replace filter, add oil if low
For mechanical grippers, check clamp cylinder pressure and travel — verify full clamp force
Verify layer is fully formed with no gaps before pickup — gaps cause vacuum leaks between products
If product surface is textured, try foam-padded vacuum cups that conform to the surface
faults.field_trick: Vacuum heads on palletizers have dozens of cups, and if even 3-4 cups are bad, the head can still pick up most of the time. But those bad cups cause intermittent drops. Run your hand over every cup with the vacuum on — you'll feel the dead ones instantly. Replace all the bad cups at once, don't chase one drop at a time.

faults.name: Palletizer Layer Squaring — Misaligned Layer
faults.description: Layer squaring plates or guides did not square the layer to the pallet footprint. Layer is skewed or offset on the pallet, risking instability of the pallet stack.
probable_causes: Squaring plate cylinder not extending fully — low air pressure or cylinder seal failure
Squaring plate guide rail bent — physical damage from product impact
Squaring plate sensor not confirming plate position — proximity sensor gap too large
Products in the layer too tightly packed — squaring plates can't compress the layer further
Layer forming produced a loose layer — products have gaps that squaring can't correct
fix_steps: Check squaring plate cylinder operation — manually trigger from PLC override
Verify air pressure at the squaring cylinders — typically 40-60 PSI
Inspect squaring plates for physical damage — straighten or replace bent plates
Check squaring plate position sensors — adjust gap to 2-3mm from target
Verify layer dimensions match the squaring plate travel — layer must fit within the squaring envelope
If layer is loose, address the layer forming issue upstream — squaring plates can correct small misalignment, not large gaps
faults.field_trick: If layers are consistently skewed in the same direction, one squaring plate is probably hitting its stroke limit before contacting the product. Increase that plate's cylinder stroke by adjusting the stop collar, or move the layer drop point slightly so the layer lands closer to center.

faults.name: Palletizer Elevator / Lift Table — Position Fault
faults.description: Pallet elevator or lift table did not reach the commanded position. Failed to index down for the next layer or failed to position at the correct height for discharge.
probable_causes: Elevator drive chain stretched — position doesn't match encoder reading
Elevator VFD faulted — overcurrent from overloaded pallet or mechanical binding
Position encoder on elevator lost signal — cable or encoder failure
Elevator guide rails binding — misalignment or lack of lubrication
Load on pallet exceeded elevator weight capacity — motor can't lift
fix_steps: Check elevator VFD for fault codes — clear and investigate root cause
Verify elevator moves freely without load — jog up and down in manual mode
Check elevator drive chain tension — adjust or replace if excessively stretched
Inspect elevator guide rails — clean and lubricate, check for binding or wear marks
Verify encoder feedback — watch position count on PLC display while jogging elevator
Check pallet weight — verify total pallet load doesn't exceed elevator rating
faults.field_trick: When an elevator indexes down for each layer, it must drop exactly one product height. If the drop distance is off by 1/4 inch per layer and there are 8 layers, the last layer is 2 inches off. This causes the top layer to slam down (too much drop) or not clear the squaring plates (too little drop). Calibrate the layer height precisely.

faults.name: Robotic Palletizer — Axis Fault / Reach Limit
faults.description: Robotic palletizer arm has faulted on an axis limit, collision detection, or servo error. Robot has stopped mid-cycle.
probable_causes: Pallet position shifted — robot trying to place at a location outside its work envelope
Servo motor overloaded — load too heavy or acceleration too aggressive
Collision detected — robot arm hit an obstacle or safety fence
Encoder feedback error on one axis — motor encoder cable or encoder failure
Robot program position taught incorrectly — waypoint is in a collision zone
fix_steps: Check robot teach pendant for specific fault code and faulted axis
If collision detection, visually inspect for physical contact — check gripper, product, and surrounding structure
Clear the fault on the teach pendant — do NOT blindly resume, check the robot position first
Jog the robot to a safe home position at slow speed before restarting the program
If axis fault, check motor and encoder cables on the faulted axis joint
Verify pallet and product positions — re-teach waypoints if pallets or products have shifted
faults.field_trick: When a robot palletizer faults on collision detection but nothing is obviously wrong, check the product weight. Collision detection works by measuring motor torque — a heavier-than-expected product registers as a collision. If product weight changed, update the payload parameter in the robot program.

faults.name: Palletizer Integrated Stretch Wrapper — Ring Fault
faults.description: Integrated stretch wrap ring or rotary arm on the palletizer wrapping station has faulted. Film is not being applied or the rotation mechanism has stopped.
probable_causes: Ring drive motor VFD faulted — overcurrent, overheat, or communication loss
Film roll empty — needs replacement
Film tail lost — film detached from the pallet during wrapping
Ring rotation obstructed — product or pallet board protruding into the ring path
Film pre-stretch unit jammed — rollers seized or film wrapped around the pre-stretch rollers
fix_steps: Check ring drive VFD for faults — clear and investigate
Load new film roll if empty — thread through pre-stretch rollers and attach to pallet
Check for obstructions in the ring rotation path — clear any protruding product or pallet material
Inspect pre-stretch rollers — clean off any wrapped film, verify rollers spin freely
Verify ring home position sensor — ring must be in the correct start position for the wrap cycle
Check film clamp/cut mechanism — verify it grabs film at the start and cuts at the end of the cycle
faults.field_trick: When film wraps around the pre-stretch rollers, don't just cut it off and restart. Stop and find out why — usually one roller is seized, making the film stick to it. Replace or free the seized roller, THEN clean the film off both rollers. Otherwise the film wraps again on the next cycle and you're right back where you started.

faults.name: Palletizer Discharge Conveyor — Full Pallet Not Clearing
faults.description: Completed pallet on the palletizer discharge conveyor is not advancing to the pickup area. Palletizer cannot start next pallet because discharge area is not clear.
probable_causes: Discharge conveyor VFD or motor faulted — check VFD display
Discharge area full — previous pallet not picked up by forklift
Pallet jammed on discharge conveyor — broken pallet board caught on conveyor frame
Discharge pallet present photo-eye blocked — detecting pallet that's not there (false full)
Transfer mechanism between palletizer and discharge conveyor failed — roller or chain drive issue
fix_steps: Check if a pallet is physically blocking the discharge — have forklift remove it
Check discharge conveyor motor/VFD — clear any faults and restart
Inspect the discharge conveyor path for pallet debris or obstructions
Verify discharge photo-eyes — clean and confirm correct operation
Check transfer mechanism — chains, rollers, or pop-up transfers that move the pallet from palletizer to discharge
If automated pallet wrapper is inline, check wrapper status — a faulted wrapper stops pallet flow
faults.field_trick: The most common cause of discharge conveyor blockage is forklift operators falling behind on pallet pickup. Before troubleshooting the conveyor, radio the forklift driver. If the pickup area has 4 pallets stacked up, the problem isn't the conveyor — it's a resourcing issue. The machines don't have the problem, the process does.

faults.name: Palletizer Hoist / Elevator Overload
faults.description: Palletizer hoist or main elevator overload protection tripped. Load on the elevator exceeds the rated capacity or the drive cannot lift the current load.
probable_causes: Pallet load exceeds the palletizer weight rating — too many layers or heavy product
Elevator mechanical friction increased — guide rails dry or misaligned
Elevator counterweight (if equipped) cable broken or out of adjustment
Hoist motor undersized for the application — motor is at its current limit
VFD current limit parameter set too low for the application
fix_steps: Check pallet weight against palletizer nameplate capacity
Lubricate elevator guide rails and check for binding
If counterweight system, check cable integrity and counterweight position
Monitor hoist motor current during lift — compare to motor FLA and VFD current limit
If VFD current limit is too low, increase to motor FLA x 1.1 (but do not exceed motor rating)
If motor is genuinely undersized, evaluate options: reduce pallet height (fewer layers) or upgrade motor
faults.field_trick: Palletizer ratings assume a specific product weight per layer. When production switches to a heavier product, nobody recalculates the pallet weight. 8 layers of 12-oz cans is a lot different than 8 layers of 20-oz bottles. Know your per-layer weight and multiply by the number of layers before assuming the palletizer can handle it.

faults.name: Palletizer Top Frame / Top Sheet Dispenser Fault
faults.description: Top frame or top sheet placement mechanism failed to apply the top frame or cap sheet to the completed pallet. Pallet is unstable for transport without the top frame.
probable_causes: Top frame magazine empty — no frames available
Vacuum pickup failed — cups worn or vacuum pump issue
Top frame jammed in the magazine — frames stuck together or misloaded
Placement mechanism position off — top frame not centered on the pallet
Top frame size wrong — different product SKU requires different frame size
fix_steps: Reload top frame magazine with correct size frames
Check vacuum cups on the pickup head — replace worn cups
Verify vacuum level — minimum 18 inches Hg at the pickup cups
Check frame magazine loading — frames should be flat and not stuck together
Adjust placement mechanism position if top frame is landing off-center
Verify correct recipe/frame size is selected for the current product run
faults.field_trick: Top frames made of corrugated board curl in humidity. When they curl, the vacuum cups can't seal on the surface. Store top frames flat in a dry area and rotate stock — first in, first out. Frames that have been sitting for months in a humid warehouse will jam the magazine and frustrate everyone.

faults.name: Palletizer Infeed Conveyor — Product Jam at Turn
faults.description: Product jammed at the palletizer infeed conveyor turn or curve. Products are not negotiating the turn cleanly, causing pileup and line stoppage.
probable_causes: Conveyor speed mismatch at the turn — upstream too fast, turn conveyor too slow
Guide rails at the turn are too tight — not enough clearance for product dimensions
Turn belt or roller surface worn — insufficient friction to push product through the turn
Product bottom surface slippery — product slides straight instead of following the curve
Turn conveyor not level — products tipping at the transition
fix_steps: Clear the jammed products from the turn area
Verify conveyor speeds — upstream, turn, and downstream should be matched or turn should be slightly faster
Adjust guide rails at the turn — provide 1/4 to 1/2 inch clearance on each side of the product
Inspect turn belt or roller surface — replace if worn smooth
Verify turn conveyor is level — shim if needed to prevent product tipping
If product is slippery, add a deflector plate or powered side-transfer belt to assist product through the turn
faults.field_trick: Product jams at conveyor turns happen more with tall, narrow products than short, wide products. The center of gravity is too high, so the product tips when it changes direction. Slow down the infeed speed by 10% before the turn — it reduces the lateral force on the product and eliminates 80% of the tipping jams.

faults.name: Palletizer Safety Zone Violation
faults.description: Safety scanner or light curtain around the palletizer detected intrusion into a restricted zone. Machine stopped and requires safety reset after the zone is clear.
probable_causes: Person entered the palletizer safety zone — intentional or accidental
Product fell off the pallet into the scanner zone — triggers same response as a person
Safety scanner lens dirty — detecting dust or condensation as an intrusion
Scanner zone configuration changed — zones shifted or sensitivity altered
Forklift or product protruding into the scanner field from the adjacent area
fix_steps: Verify no person is in the palletizer safety zone — walk the zone completely before resetting
Remove any product, pallet debris, or objects that fell into the scanner zone
Clean safety scanner lens — use lint-free cloth, no solvents
Check scanner zone configuration — verify zones match the original safety design
After clearing the zone, press the safety reset button — most systems require a deliberate manual reset
If scanner trips repeatedly with no apparent cause, check for reflective surfaces, steam, or extreme dust that may trigger the scanner
faults.field_trick: Safety scanners detect objects by measuring reflected laser light distance. A pile of stretch wrap or a shiny piece of metal on the floor can confuse the scanner into thinking there's an object. When you have nuisance trips, look at the scanner diagnostic display — it shows you exactly which zone and angle detected the intrusion. Walk to that spot and look for shiny or reflective debris.

faults.name: Light Curtain Fault — Beam Blocked or Misaligned
faults.description: Safety light curtain has one or more beams permanently blocked or misaligned. Machine cannot reset because the light curtain does not show all-clear.
probable_causes: Physical object blocking one or more beams — product, debris, or tool left in the curtain field
Emitter or receiver lens dirty — dust, overspray, or condensation
Light curtain knocked out of alignment — vibration or impact shifted one column
One or more LEDs in the emitter failed — dead beams in the curtain
Sunlight or high-intensity ambient light overwhelming the receiver — curtain near dock door or window
fix_steps: Clear any objects from the light curtain detection field — walk the full width of the curtain
Clean emitter and receiver lenses with lint-free cloth — both columns
Check alignment — most light curtains have alignment LEDs on both emitter and receiver, all should be solid green
If one beam is permanently blocked but field is clear, the corresponding LED in the emitter may have failed — check beam-by-beam status
If ambient light interference, install light curtain shrouds or hoods to block direct sunlight
Verify wiring to light curtain controller — check for loose connections on the safety circuit
faults.field_trick: When a light curtain won't reset but the field looks clear, bend down and look through the curtain at floor level. A small piece of product or a pallet board on the floor can block a bottom beam that you can't see from standing height. The bottom beams are the most commonly blocked because debris collects at floor level.

faults.name: Safety Laser Scanner — Zone Violation Warning
faults.description: Safety laser scanner detected an object in the warning zone. If the object enters the safety (stop) zone, the machine will stop. Warning zone provides an early alert.
probable_causes: Person approaching the machine — warning zone functioning as designed
Product or material encroaching on the scanner zone — pallet, cart, or product overflow
Scanner zone configuration too large for the workspace — warning zone extends into a walkway
Scanner lens dirty — dust causing false readings at the edge of the zone
Reflective surface near the scanner — mirror, metal plate, or water puddle bouncing the beam
fix_steps: Identify what is in the warning zone — person, product, or environmental factor
If workspace layout has changed, re-evaluate scanner zone configuration to eliminate nuisance warnings
Clean scanner lens — use lint-free cloth gently
Check for reflective surfaces that may be confusing the scanner — cover or remove them
Access scanner configuration software to view the zone map and identify which zone segment is triggered
If nuisance trips are frequent, consider adjusting the warning zone boundaries (safety zone must NOT be reduced without a risk assessment)
faults.field_trick: Safety scanner warning zones can be changed without a safety review. Safety (stop) zones CANNOT. If operators complain about nuisance warnings, you can safely reduce the warning zone boundary. But NEVER reduce the safety zone without a full risk assessment and documentation update. The warning zone is for convenience; the safety zone is for saving lives.

faults.name: Emergency Stop Circuit — Station Unknown
faults.description: Emergency stop circuit is open but the specific station that was pressed cannot be identified from the control system. One or more E-stop buttons are engaged somewhere in the circuit.
probable_causes: An E-stop was pressed at a remote station — operator cannot identify which one
An E-stop button is mechanically stuck in the pressed position — spring or latch failure
E-stop wiring has a broken wire — circuit reads as open (same as pressed), fail-safe behavior
E-stop contact block failed open — contact does not close when button is released
Series-wired E-stop circuit makes individual station identification difficult
fix_steps: Walk to every E-stop station on the machine and check each button — look for buttons in the pressed (down/locked) position
At each station, twist the E-stop mushroom head to release — if it pops up, that station was pressed
If all E-stops appear released but circuit is still open, use a multimeter to trace the circuit station by station
Check each E-stop for mechanical damage — buttons that won't pop up need replacement
On series-wired circuits, disconnect each station one at a time and jumper across to isolate the faulted station
After finding and clearing the E-stop, press the safety reset button to re-enable the machine
faults.field_trick: For machines with 10+ E-stop stations in series, put numbered labels on every E-stop and add individual E-stop status inputs to the PLC. Yes, it takes more wiring and I/O points, but being able to see on the HMI 'E-Stop Station 7 Active' saves 30 minutes of walking the machine every time someone bumps a button. Install it once, save time forever.

faults.name: Safety Interlock — Trapped Key System Fault
faults.description: Trapped key interlock system fault. Key is not in the expected position, or the lock exchange mechanism has failed. Machine cannot start because the safety key sequence is incomplete.
probable_causes: Operator has the key — key was removed from the lock box and not returned
Key is in the wrong lock — keys swapped between stations
Lock mechanism jammed — key won't turn or can't be inserted
Key broken in the lock — fragment stuck in the cylinder
Lock switch (if monitored electrically) not confirming key presence
fix_steps: Locate all keys in the trapped key sequence — verify each key is in its correct lock position
If a key is missing, find the person who has it — the key represents a person's safety lockout
If a key is in the wrong lock, return it to the correct position following the designed key exchange sequence
If a lock is jammed, do NOT force it — use graphite lubricant in the cylinder
If a key is broken in a lock, lock out the entire system and call the trapped key system manufacturer for extraction
Do NOT bypass trapped key systems — they enforce a physical safety procedure sequence
faults.field_trick: Trapped key systems get jammed when people use the wrong key or force keys that don't quite fit. Apply a small amount of graphite (not WD-40 or oil) to every key and lock once a year. Graphite lubricates without attracting dust. Oil in a lock cylinder attracts grit and causes the lock to jam worse over time.

faults.name: Guard Monitoring Relay — Channel Fault
faults.description: Guard monitoring safety relay detected a channel fault. Dual-channel monitoring of guard switches has a discrepancy or one channel is not reading correctly.
probable_causes: One guard switch contact failed — one channel reads differently from the other
Wiring fault on one channel — broken wire, loose terminal, or short to ground
Guard switch actuator misaligned — one channel actuates but the other doesn't
Relay input module failed on one channel — only receiving signal from one input
Discrepancy timer expired — too much time between channel transitions
fix_steps: Check guard monitoring relay LED indicators — identify which channel is faulted
Test the guard switch — open and close the guard slowly, watch both channel LEDs on the relay
If one channel doesn't respond, check wiring from that channel back to the relay terminals
Test guard switch contacts with multimeter — both NC and NO contacts must operate correctly
Replace guard switch if one contact is dead — do not bypass the failed channel
After repair, reset the monitoring relay per manufacturer procedure
faults.field_trick: When a guard monitoring relay trips on channel discrepancy, it means one contact is slower than the other. It doesn't mean the switch is dead — it means it's DYING. Replace it now. A switch that trips discrepancy today will fail completely next week, and a complete failure is harder to diagnose because you lose the error code.

faults.name: E-Stop Circuit Monitoring — Ground Fault Detected
faults.description: Safety circuit monitoring has detected a ground fault on the E-stop circuit wiring. This compromises the safety integrity of the E-stop loop.
probable_causes: E-stop wiring insulation damaged — cable pinched in conduit, panel entry, or by sharp edge
Moisture in an E-stop junction box — water ingress causing leakage to ground
E-stop cable run through an area with chemical exposure — insulation degraded
Incorrect wiring — safety circuit wire accidentally connected to earth ground
Conduit fill too tight — wires compressed causing insulation breakdown
fix_steps: Disconnect the E-stop circuit from the safety relay and megger the circuit wiring to ground
If megger shows low resistance, isolate circuit sections to find the ground fault location
Inspect all junction boxes and cable entry points in the E-stop circuit for moisture or damage
Check cable routing for pinch points, sharp edges, or chemical exposure
Repair or replace damaged cable sections — use cable rated for the environment
After repair, megger the complete circuit to confirm >1 Mohm insulation resistance to ground
faults.field_trick: Ground faults on safety circuits are sneaky because they might not trip the E-stop — they just degrade the safety integrity. A ground fault can mask a real E-stop press, making it look like the circuit is fine when it's not. Treat every safety circuit ground fault as urgent, even if the machine still runs fine.

faults.name: Magnetic Safety Switch — Door Not Detected
faults.description: Coded magnetic safety switch is not detecting the door-mounted magnet. Guard door appears closed but the safety switch does not confirm the closed position.
probable_causes: Door or gate has sagged — magnet and switch no longer aligned
Magnet has weakened or shifted in its mounting — no longer in sensing range
Switch sensing gap too large — switch requires very close proximity (typically <15mm)
Switch has been tampered with — magnet taped to the switch to bypass the door
Switch wiring disconnected — cable cut or terminal loose
fix_steps: Check door/gate alignment — verify the magnet passes within the switch's rated sensing range when closed
Measure the gap between magnet and switch with the door closed — adjust mounting to achieve required gap
Check for tampering — there should NOT be a magnet taped or mounted permanently on the switch body
Verify switch operation — close the door and check the PLC input LED changes state
Check switch wiring — trace cable from switch to safety relay or PLC safety input
Replace switch if it does not respond even with the magnet at minimum gap — internal reed or Hall effect element may have failed
faults.field_trick: Coded magnetic switches use a specific magnet pattern to prevent bypass with a random magnet. If you replace the switch, you MUST use the matching magnet — a standard magnet from the hardware store won't work. Always keep matched pairs (switch + magnet) in your spare parts stock.

faults.name: Safety Relay — Contact Welding Detected
faults.description: Safety relay self-monitoring detected that output contacts have welded closed. Relay cannot de-energize the safety outputs. Safety integrity is compromised.
probable_causes: Relay switching capacity exceeded — load current exceeds the relay contact rating
Inductive load (contactor coil) without suppression creating arc on contact opening
Contact material degraded — high cycle count causing surface erosion and eventual welding
Short circuit on the relay output — caused high current through the contacts
Relay reaching end of life — B10d life cycle limit
fix_steps: IMMEDIATELY stop the machine — welded contacts mean the safety system CANNOT stop the machine in an emergency
Replace the safety relay immediately — do NOT attempt to repair or free welded contacts
After replacing the relay, investigate and address the root cause of the welding
If load current is close to relay rating, install an interposing contactor — relay drives the contactor coil, contactor switches the motor
Add arc suppression (RC snubber or diode) across inductive loads switched by the safety relay
Document the failure and replacement in the safety maintenance log
faults.field_trick: Safety relays with welded contacts are a ZERO tolerance condition. The relay manufacturers design them to detect this condition because a welded relay CANNOT stop the machine in an emergency. Every safety relay with this condition must be replaced immediately — not at the next shutdown, not tomorrow, NOW. Document it as a near-miss safety event.

faults.name: Safety Muting — Mute Condition Not Met
faults.description: Safety muting sequence for a light curtain or safety device did not complete correctly. Muting sensors did not detect product in the correct sequence to allow product to pass through the light curtain without stopping the machine.
probable_causes: Muting sensor alignment wrong — sensors not detecting the product at the correct position
Product orientation or size changed — muting sequence timing doesn't match new product
Muting sensor failed — one of the two required muting sensors is not working
Muting sensor wiring error — sensors wired to wrong inputs
Product speed changed — muting timing window too short or too long for current conveyor speed
fix_steps: Check both muting sensors — both must activate in the correct sequence and timing for muting to engage
Verify muting sensor alignment — they must detect the product, not each other (cross-beam or parallel beam configuration)
Check muting LED on the safety controller — should illuminate only when both conditions are met
If product size changed, readjust muting sensor positions to detect the new product at the correct conveyor position
Verify muting timeout parameter — if product takes too long between sensors, muting cancels
Check that muting lamp (if equipped) illuminates when muting is active — required by most safety standards
faults.field_trick: Muting requires EXACTLY two sensors to activate in a specific sequence and timing window. If someone adds a third sensor 'for safety,' it actually breaks the muting logic. Muting is designed with exactly two sensors to prevent bypass — one person can't hold both sensors at the same time from outside the danger zone. Don't add extra sensors without understanding the safety logic.

faults.name: Safety System — SIL/PLe Proof Test Required
faults.description: Safety system component has reached its scheduled proof test interval. Performance level (PLe) or Safety Integrity Level (SIL) requires periodic testing to maintain safety integrity.
probable_causes: Scheduled proof test interval reached — time-based maintenance item
Safety PLC or relay has internal timer tracking operational hours
Plant safety audit identified overdue proof testing
New safety regulation requires more frequent testing than previously scheduled
After any safety component replacement, proof test is required to validate the installation
fix_steps: Schedule the proof test during a planned downtime — test requires intentional safety device activation
Follow the machine-specific proof test procedure — this is a documented procedure, not improvised
Test each safety device: press each E-stop, open each guard door, interrupt each light curtain — verify machine stops
Verify all safety devices reset correctly after testing
Test redundant/dual-channel systems by faulting each channel independently — both must independently stop the machine
Document the proof test results, date, and tester name — this is required for safety audit compliance
Reset the proof test timer in the safety PLC/relay if applicable
faults.field_trick: Proof testing is where you find the safety system failures that normal operation hides. A machine can run for years with one channel of a dual-channel E-stop circuit broken — the other channel keeps working. The proof test catches this. Never skip proof tests just because the machine seems fine. Fine is not safe.
